days.title: L'alfabeto e i suoni italiani
days.objective: Read any Italian word aloud with the five pure vowel sounds and know which letters belong to the alphabet.
days.paretoFocus: Italian spelling is almost perfectly regular, so learning the vowels once lets you pronounce thousands of new words correctly without ever hearing them first.
drills: Say the five vowels aloud ten times, keeping each one short and pure with no glide at the end.
Read these aloud slowly, one syllable at a time: la casa, il mare, il libro, il sole, la luna.
Write out the five vowels and next to each one write an Italian word you already know that contains it.

items: How many letters are in the standard Italian alphabet, and which five appear only in foreign words?
Which English habit must you drop when you say the Italian o and e?
Say the Italian words for house, sea, book, sun and moon with their articles.

activities.title: Le cinque vocali
activities.intro: The standard Italian alphabet has 21 letters. The letters j, k, w, x and y appear only in words taken from other languages. Italian has five vowels and each one keeps a single pure sound, never sliding into a second sound the way English vowels do.

items.hr: la casa
items.en: the house
items.note: a is open and clear, like the a in father

items.hr: il mare
items.en: the sea
items.note: e is short and pure, never the glide of English day

items.hr: il libro
items.en: the book
items.note: i sounds like the ee in see

items.hr: il sole
items.en: the sun
items.note: o stays a single sound, never oh-oo

items.hr: la luna
items.en: the moon
items.note: u sounds like the oo in food, never like you

activities.title: Lettere e vocali
activities.intro: Check what you know about the Italian alphabet and its vowel sounds.

questions.prompt: How many letters does the standard Italian alphabet have?
questions.explanation: Italian uses 21 letters. The other five Latin letters are only used in borrowed words.

questions.prompt: Which group of letters appears in Italian only in words borrowed from other languages?
questions.explanation: These five letters are not part of the native Italian alphabet and turn up mainly in names and loanwords.

questions.prompt: Type the Italian word for the sea, together with its article.
questions.explanation: Mare is masculine, so it takes il.

questions.prompt: Type the Italian word for the moon, together with its article.
questions.explanation: Luna ends in -a and is feminine, so it takes la.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'The sun is beautiful.'
questions.explanation: Italian keeps subject, verb, adjective order here, and the adjective bello agrees with the masculine singular sole.

activities.title: Ripeti con me
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend coaches your pronunciation, so keep every vowel short and pure.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ciao! Ripeti: a, e, i, o, u.
lines.en: Hi! Repeat: a, e, i, o, u.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: A, e, i, o, u.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Good. Now: the house.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: The house.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Perfect. And the sun?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: The sun.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Well done! Last word: the moon.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: The moon. Thank you!

days.title: C e G, dolci e dure
days.objective: Pronounce and spell c and g correctly before every vowel, and know what the letters h and i are doing inside words like chi and ciao.
days.paretoFocus: This one system decides how you read a huge share of Italian words, and getting it wrong turns cena into a word nobody recognises.
drills: Say these pairs aloud and listen for the switch: casa and cena, gatto and gelato, cubo and ciao.
Cover the notes and read aloud: chi, che, spaghetti, giorno, ciao, cucina.
Write five words you know with a hard c or g and five with a soft c or g, then check the letter after them.

items: Which two vowels make c and g soft?
Which letter is inserted to keep the sound hard, and which letter keeps it soft before a, o and u?
Say aloud, then spell: the dinner, the ice cream, the cat, hi.

activities.title: Suoni dolci e suoni duri
activities.intro: C and g are soft before e and i, and hard before a, o and u. To keep the sound hard before e or i, Italian inserts an h. To keep the sound soft before a, o or u, it inserts an i, and that i is not pronounced.

items.hr: la cena
items.en: the dinner
items.note: c plus e is soft, like the ch in chair

items.hr: il gelato
items.en: the ice cream
items.note: g plus e is soft, like the j in jam

items.hr: il gatto
items.en: the cat
items.note: before a, o and u the sound is hard: casa, gatto, cubo

items.hr: gli spaghetti
items.en: spaghetti
items.note: the h keeps the sound hard: chi, che, spaghetti

items.hr: ciao
items.en: hi, or bye
items.note: the i keeps the sound soft and is not pronounced: ciao, giorno

activities.title: Dolce o duro?
activities.intro: Practise deciding whether c and g are soft or hard, and which letter makes them that way.

questions.prompt: In which word is the letter c soft, like the ch in chair?
questions.explanation: C is soft only before e and i, and cena has an e straight after the c.

questions.prompt: Which letter does Italian insert to keep c and g hard before e and i?
questions.explanation: The h has no sound of its own; its job is to block the softening, as in che, chi and spaghetti.

questions.prompt: Type the Italian word for ice cream, together with its article.
questions.explanation: Gelato is masculine and its g is soft because an e follows.

questions.prompt: Type the Italian question word meaning who, spelled so that the c stays hard.
questions.explanation: Without the h it would be ci, with a soft sound. The h keeps the k sound.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'the cat and the ice cream'.
questions.explanation: Each noun keeps its own article, and e simply joins them.

activities.title: Al bar
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You order at a bar, and almost every word tests a soft or hard c or g.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Buongiorno! Un caffè o un cappuccino?
lines.en: Good morning! A coffee or a cappuccino?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: A cappuccino, thank you.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: And an ice cream?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, an ice cream, thank you.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ecco il gelato e il cappuccino.
lines.en: Here is the ice cream and the cappuccino.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Thank you! How good it is!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: You are welcome. Have a good day!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Thanks, bye!

days.title: Gli, gn, sc e le doppie
days.objective: Pronounce the clusters gli, gn and sc correctly, and hear and hold double consonants, which change the meaning of a word.
days.paretoFocus: Double consonants are not decoration: nonno and nono are different words, so ignoring them makes you hard to understand and costs marks in a written exam.
drills: Say each pair twice, holding the double letter clearly: nonno and nono, cappello and capello, sette and sete.
Read aloud: la famiglia, il bagno, il pesce, la scuola, lo gnocco.
Say one word from a pair aloud and have a partner, or your own recording, tell you which one you said.

items: How is gn pronounced, and what English sound is closest to it?
When does sc sound like sh, and when is it hard?
Give the meaning of both words in each pair: nonno and nono, cappello and capello.

activities.title: Gruppi difficili e consonanti doppie
activities.intro: Three clusters need attention: gli sounds like the lli in million, gn sounds like the ny in canyon, and sc sounds like sh before e and i but stays hard before a, o and u. A double consonant is held roughly twice as long as a single one, and that length carries meaning.

items.hr: la famiglia
items.en: the family
items.note: gli is one soft sound, never a hard g plus l

items.hr: il bagno
items.en: the bathroom
items.note: gn is the ny of canyon, in a single syllable

items.hr: il pesce
items.en: the fish
items.note: sc plus e or i sounds like sh, but la scuola is hard

items.hr: il nonno
items.en: the grandfather
items.note: hold the nn; il nono with one n means the ninth

items.hr: il cappello
items.en: the hat
items.note: il capello with one p means the strand of hair

activities.title: Doppie e suoni speciali
activities.intro: Practise the clusters and prove that you can spell double consonants exactly.

questions.prompt: Which spelling means the grandfather?
questions.explanation: Two n letters give grandfather; il nono with one n means the ninth.

questions.prompt: How is gn pronounced in the word bagno?
options: like the g in go followed by n
questions.explanation: Gn is a single soft sound made with the tongue against the palate.

questions.prompt: Type the Italian word for the hat, with its article, and watch the double consonant.
questions.explanation: Two p letters give hat; with one p the word means a hair.

questions.prompt: Type the Italian number word for 7.
questions.explanation: Two t letters. With one t, sete means thirst.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'the fish and the family'.
questions.explanation: Pesce is masculine and takes il, famiglia is feminine and takes la.

activities.title: Una parola o l'altra
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend tests whether you can hear the difference a double consonant makes.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ecco la famiglia: il nonno e la nonna.
lines.en: Here is the family: the grandfather and the grandmother.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Il nonno e la nonna. Che bello!
lines.en: The grandfather and the grandmother. How lovely!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E questo? Il cappello o il capello?
lines.en: And this one? The hat or the hair?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: The hat, with two p letters.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Well done. Now: seven or thirst?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Seven, with two t letters.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Perfect. Last word: the bathroom.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: The bathroom, with gn.

days.title: Salutare e presentarsi
days.objective: Greet someone at any time of day, say your name, react to an introduction and say goodbye.
days.paretoFocus: These fixed phrases open and close every single conversation you will ever have in Italian, and they are the first thing anyone judges you on.
drills: Say the right greeting out loud for these times: 9 in the morning, 6 in the evening, just before bed.
Introduce yourself out loud five times, changing only the name each time.
Practise the closing pair: say goodbye once informally and once politely.

items: Which greeting works both on arriving and on leaving, and only with people you address informally?
What single word do Italians say the moment they are introduced to someone?
Say your own name in Italian and then ask the other person for theirs.

activities.title: Ciao e buongiorno
activities.intro: Italian greetings change with the time of day and with how formal you are being. Ciao is informal and serves for both hello and goodbye, while buongiorno, buonasera and arrivederci are neutral or polite. Learn mi chiamo as one fixed block for now.

items.hr: Buongiorno!
items.en: Good morning!
items.note: used from waking up until the late afternoon

items.hr: Buonasera!
items.en: Good evening!
items.note: from the late afternoon on; buonanotte only when leaving to sleep

items.hr: Ciao!
items.en: Hi! or Bye!
items.note: informal, and it works both on arrival and on leaving

items.hr: Mi chiamo Anna. E tu?
items.en: My name is Anna. And you?
items.note: learn mi chiamo whole; the grammar behind it comes later

items.hr: Piacere! Arrivederci.
items.en: Nice to meet you! Goodbye.
items.note: arrivederci is the polite goodbye, usable with anyone

activities.title: Saluti giusti
activities.intro: Choose the greeting that fits the moment and the person.

questions.prompt: You walk into a shop at nine in the morning. Which greeting fits?
questions.explanation: Buongiorno covers the whole first half of the day; buonanotte is only for going to sleep.

questions.prompt: Which greeting can you use with a friend both when you arrive and when you leave?
questions.explanation: Ciao is the informal all purpose greeting, used in both directions.

questions.prompt: Someone has just told you their name. Type the one word Italians say at that moment.
questions.explanation: Piacere literally means pleasure and works as nice to meet you.

questions.prompt: Type the polite Italian goodbye you would use when leaving a doctor's office.
questions.explanation: Ciao would be too informal with someone you do not know well.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'My name is Marco. And you?'
questions.explanation: Mi chiamo stays together as a block, and E tu? hands the question back.

activities.title: Un primo incontro
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You meet someone for the first time and take the introduction from hello to goodbye.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Good morning!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Good morning!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: My name is Giulia. And you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: My name is Marco.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Nice to meet you, Marco!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Nice to meet you!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: See you tomorrow, Marco. Goodbye!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Goodbye, Giulia. Bye!

days.title: Il verbo essere
days.objective: Use all six present tense forms of essere to say who you are, where you are and what you are like.
days.paretoFocus: Essere is the most frequent verb in Italian, and with it alone you can already give your name, nationality, location and mood.
drills: Say the six forms aloud in order twice, then say them backwards from loro to io.
Make five true sentences about yourself and people you know, one for each different form.
Write the same sentence about a man and about a woman, changing the adjective ending each time.

items: Which two persons share the identical form sono?
Which form of essere carries a written accent, and why does the accent matter?
Say in Italian: I am Italian, we are tired, they are here.

activities.title: Essere al presente
activities.intro: Essere means to be. Its forms are sono, sei, è, siamo, siete, sono. Note that io and loro share the same form, sono, and that the third person è always carries an accent, because e without the accent means and.

items.hr: Io sono italiano.
items.en: I am Italian.
items.note: io sono; the pronoun is usually dropped in real speech

items.hr: Tu sei simpatica.
items.en: You are nice.
items.note: simpatica has an -a because it describes a woman

items.hr: Maria è a casa.
items.en: Maria is at home.
items.note: lui and lei both take è, written with the accent

items.hr: Noi siamo stanchi.
items.en: We are tired.
items.note: stanchi takes an h to keep the c hard in the plural

items.hr: Voi siete pronti e loro sono qui.
items.en: You are ready and they are here.
items.note: siete for voi, sono for loro, the same word as for io

activities.title: Sono, sei, è
activities.intro: Pick and type the right form of essere for each subject.

questions.prompt: Complete correctly: Marco ___ a Roma.
questions.explanation: Marco is a third person singular subject, so the verb is è, with the accent.

questions.prompt: Which sentence pairs the subject with the right verb form?
questions.explanation: Noi takes siamo; siete belongs to voi.

questions.prompt: Complete with essere: Io ___ contento.
questions.explanation: The io form of essere is sono, which loro also uses.

questions.prompt: Complete with essere: Voi ___ in ritardo.
questions.explanation: Voi, addressing more than one person, takes siete.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'We are Italian.'
questions.explanation: The adjective italiani is plural and masculine to match noi.

activities.title: Di dove sei?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You compare nationalities and plans, using a different form of essere in almost every line.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Hi! Are you Italian?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: No, io sono spagnolo. E tu?
lines.en: No, I am Spanish. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: I am Italian, from Florence.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Florence is beautiful!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sì, molto. Voi siete qui per una settimana?
lines.en: Yes, very. Are you here for a week?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: No, noi siamo qui per tre giorni.
lines.en: No, we are here for three days.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: And the friends? Are they here?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, they are at the hotel.

days.title: Il verbo avere
days.objective: Use the six present tense forms of avere to say what you own, and to express age, hunger, thirst and cold the Italian way.
days.paretoFocus: Avere covers possession plus a whole family of everyday states where English uses to be, so it fixes the single most common beginner mistake about age.
drills: Say the six forms aloud, then say each one again without pronouncing the h.
Say your own age in Italian, then the age of two people in your family.
Say four states using avere: hungry, thirsty, hot, cold.

items: Which forms of avere are written with an h, and what does that h sound like?
Which verb do you use for age in Italian, and what is the whole sentence for being thirty?
Say in Italian: we have a dog, they are cold, do you have a brother?

activities.title: Avere al presente
activities.intro: Avere means to have: ho, hai, ha, abbiamo, avete, hanno. The h is written but never pronounced; it exists only to keep these forms apart from other short words. Italian also uses avere for age and for physical states where English uses to be.

items.hr: Io ho una sorella.
items.en: I have a sister.
items.note: ho is pronounced exactly like the letter o

items.hr: Tu hai fame?
items.en: Are you hungry?
items.note: avere fame, literally to have hunger

items.hr: Lei ha trent'anni.
items.en: She is thirty years old.
items.note: age takes avere, never essere; trenta drops its a before anni

items.hr: Noi abbiamo un cane.
items.en: We have a dog.
items.note: abbiamo has no h but does have a double b

items.hr: Voi avete sete e loro hanno freddo.
items.en: You are thirsty and they are cold.
items.note: avete without h, hanno with h and a double n

activities.title: Ho, hai, ha
activities.intro: Match each subject to the right form of avere, including the expressions of age and hunger.

questions.prompt: Complete correctly: Loro ___ due gatti.
questions.explanation: Loro takes hanno, written with h and a double n.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I am twenty years old' in Italian?
questions.explanation: Age uses avere, and venti loses its final vowel before anni.

questions.prompt: Complete with avere: Noi ___ un appartamento a Milano.
questions.explanation: The noi form is abbiamo, with a double b and no h.

questions.prompt: Complete with avere: Tu ___ un fratello?
questions.explanation: The tu form is hai, and the h is silent.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I have a dog and a cat.'
questions.explanation: Each noun needs its own indefinite article, and the subject pronoun can be dropped.

activities.title: Fame e sete
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a friend sort out food and drink, using avere in every line.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Are you hungry?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, ho molta fame. E tu?
lines.en: Yes, I am very hungry. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: I am thirsty. Do you have water?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, we have water and bread.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Perfect. Does Marco have a sandwich?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, lui ha un panino grande.
lines.en: Yes, he has a big sandwich.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: And are the boys hungry?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, hanno molta fame e sete.
lines.en: Yes, they are very hungry and thirsty.

days.title: Maschile e femminile
days.objective: Predict the gender of an Italian noun from its ending and store every new noun together with its article.
days.paretoFocus: Gender drives articles, adjectives and pronouns, so learning a noun without its gender means getting every later word in the sentence wrong.
drills: Sort ten nouns you already know into two columns, masculine and feminine, using their endings.
Say each of these aloud with the right article: casa, libro, ristorante, chiave, studente.
Pick three new nouns you meet today and write each one down with its article, never on its own.

items: Which ending usually signals a masculine noun, and which one a feminine noun?
Why are nouns ending in -e a special case?
Which article goes before a masculine noun starting with s plus a consonant, and which before a vowel?

activities.title: Il genere dei nomi
activities.intro: Every Italian noun is either masculine or feminine. Nouns ending in -o are usually masculine and nouns ending in -a are usually feminine, but nouns ending in -e can be either, so you must learn them with their article. Nouns ending in -zione are always feminine.

items.hr: il libro
items.en: the book
items.note: most nouns in -o are masculine

items.hr: la casa
items.en: the house
items.note: most nouns in -a are feminine

items.hr: il ristorante
items.en: the restaurant
items.note: an -e noun that happens to be masculine

items.hr: la chiave
items.en: the key
items.note: another -e noun, this one feminine; you cannot guess, so learn the article

items.hr: lo studente e l'amica
items.en: the student and the female friend
items.note: lo before s plus consonant, l' before a vowel

activities.title: Maschile o femminile?
activities.intro: Decide the gender of each noun and give it the right article.

questions.prompt: Which of these nouns is feminine?
questions.explanation: Nouns ending in -zione are always feminine, so they take la.

questions.prompt: Nouns ending in -e in Italian are:
questions.explanation: The -e ending gives no clue about gender, which is why the article must be memorised with the word.

questions.prompt: Type the definite article that belongs before the noun casa.
questions.explanation: Casa ends in -a and is feminine.

questions.prompt: Type the definite article that belongs before the noun ristorante.
questions.explanation: Ristorante is one of the masculine -e nouns, so it takes il.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'The house is big.'
questions.explanation: Grande is one of the adjectives with a single form for both genders in the singular.

activities.title: La casa nuova
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Someone shows you around a flat, and each room comes with its own gendered article.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ecco la casa. Questa è la cucina.
lines.en: Here is the house. This is the kitchen.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Bella! E questo è il bagno?
lines.en: Lovely! And is this the bathroom?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sì. E lì c'è lo studio.
lines.en: Yes. And over there is the study.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Perfect. And the key?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: The key is here.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Thank you. Is the garden big?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sì, il giardino è molto grande.
lines.en: Yes, the garden is very big.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: What a lovely house!

days.title: Il plurale dei nomi
days.objective: Form the plural of regular nouns by changing the final vowel, and spell the plurals that need an extra h.
days.paretoFocus: Italian builds plurals by changing the ending, not by adding s, so this one habit change stops the most audible beginner error of all.
drills: Take five singular nouns you know and say each one again in the plural, with its article.
Say aloud the pairs banca and banche, amico and amici, and notice where the sound changes.
Write the plural of three -e nouns and check that both genders end in -i.

items: What do masculine -o nouns and feminine -a nouns become in the plural?
What happens to nouns ending in -e, whatever their gender?
Why does the plural of amica need an h while the plural of amico does not?

activities.title: Dal singolare al plurale
activities.intro: Italian never adds an s. Masculine nouns in -o become -i, feminine nouns in -a become -e, and nouns in -e become -i whatever their gender. When the plural ending would soften a hard c or g, an h is written to protect the sound.

items.hr: il libro, i libri
items.en: the book, the books
items.note: masculine -o becomes -i, and il becomes i

items.hr: la casa, le case
items.en: the house, the houses
items.note: feminine -a becomes -e, and la becomes le

items.hr: il ristorante, i ristoranti
items.en: the restaurant, the restaurants
items.note: -e becomes -i for both genders

items.hr: l'amico, gli amici
items.en: the friend, the friends
items.note: the spelling is regular, but the c now sounds soft

items.hr: la banca, le banche
items.en: the bank, the banks
items.note: an h is added so that the c stays hard

activities.title: Uno o tanti
activities.intro: Turn singulars into plurals and watch the spelling of the hard sounds.

questions.prompt: What is the plural of la chiave?
questions.explanation: Nouns in -e go to -i, and the feminine article la becomes le.

questions.prompt: Why is the plural amiche spelled with an h?
questions.explanation: Without the h, ce would be pronounced soft, so the h protects the original sound.

questions.prompt: Type the plural of il tavolo, with its article.
questions.explanation: Masculine -o goes to -i and il becomes i.

questions.prompt: Type the plural of la stazione, with its article.
questions.explanation: The -e ending becomes -i even though the noun is feminine, and la becomes le.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'The books are here.'
questions.explanation: The plural subject i libri takes the plural verb sono.

activities.title: In libreria
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You collect several things at once, so almost every noun turns up in the plural.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Good morning! Here are the books.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Thank you. Are they Italian books?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sì. E le riviste sono qui.
lines.en: Yes. And the magazines are here.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Good. Are the magazines new?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sì, sono nuove. Hai le chiavi?
lines.en: Yes, they are new. Do you have the keys?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, ho le chiavi e due borse.
lines.en: Yes, I have the keys and two bags.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Perfetto. Le banche sono aperte oggi?
lines.en: Perfect. Are the banks open today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, the banks are open.

days.title: Gli articoli determinativi
days.objective: Choose between il, lo, l', la in the singular and i, gli, le in the plural, based on the sound that starts the noun.
days.paretoFocus: The definite article appears in almost every Italian sentence, and picking the wrong one is instantly noticeable to any native listener.
drills: Say aloud with the right article: zaino, studente, albergo, ragazzo, ora.
Take the same five nouns and say them again in the plural with i, gli or le.
List every noun you meet today that starts with s plus a consonant, z, gn or ps, and give it lo.

items: Which beginnings force the article lo instead of il?
What is the plural of lo and of l' in the masculine?
Which single article covers every feminine plural noun?

activities.title: Il, lo, la, i, gli, le
activities.intro: The masculine article is il before most consonants, lo before s plus consonant, z, gn, ps, x and y, and l' before a vowel. Their plurals are i for il and gli for both lo and l'. The feminine is simpler: la, or l' before a vowel, and le in the plural.

items.hr: il ragazzo, i ragazzi
items.en: the boy, the boys
items.note: il before ordinary consonants, plural i

items.hr: lo zaino, gli zaini
items.en: the backpack, the backpacks
items.note: lo before z, plural gli

items.hr: lo studente, gli studenti
items.en: the student, the students
items.note: lo before s plus another consonant

items.hr: l'albergo, gli alberghi
items.en: the hotel, the hotels
items.note: l' before a vowel, and its plural is also gli

items.hr: la scuola, le scuole; l'ora, le ore
items.en: the school, the schools; the hour, the hours
items.note: feminine la, l' before a vowel, and always le in the plural

activities.title: Quale articolo?
activities.intro: Pick the article that the first sound of the noun demands.

questions.prompt: Which singular article goes with the noun zaino?
questions.explanation: A noun starting with z takes lo, never il.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct plural article: ___ amici sono italiani.
questions.explanation: Masculine nouns starting with a vowel take l' in the singular and gli in the plural.

questions.prompt: Type the singular definite article for the masculine noun sport, which begins with s plus a consonant.
questions.explanation: S plus consonant is exactly the environment that requires lo.

questions.prompt: Type the plural form of la, as used before the feminine noun ragazze.
questions.explanation: Every feminine plural noun takes the same article, whatever its first sound.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'The backpacks are new.'
questions.explanation: Zaini takes gli, and nuovi is masculine plural to agree.

activities.title: A scuola
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A short exchange at a school where every noun needs its correct article.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Good morning! Are the students here?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, gli studenti sono in classe.
lines.en: Yes, the students are in class.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: And the black backpack?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: The black backpack is here.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bene. Le lezioni sono lunghe oggi?
lines.en: Good. Are the lessons long today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, the lessons are long.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: And is the teacher Italian?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, the teacher is Italian.

days.title: Gli articoli indeterminativi
days.objective: Use un, uno, una and un' correctly, and know that only the feminine form takes an apostrophe.
days.paretoFocus: You need an indefinite article every time you order something, buy something or mention a new thing, and the apostrophe rule is a classic exam trap.
drills: Say aloud with the right indefinite article: libro, amico, studente, casa, amica.
Order three things out loud, each one with its indefinite article.
Write two sentences with a masculine vowel noun and two with a feminine vowel noun, checking the apostrophe.

items: Which indefinite article goes before a masculine noun starting with s plus a consonant or with z?
Why is there no apostrophe in un amico but there is one in un'amica?
Say in Italian: a house, a student, a backpack, a female friend.

activities.title: Un, uno, una, un'
activities.intro: The masculine indefinite article is un before most nouns, including those starting with a vowel, and uno before s plus consonant, z, gn and ps. The feminine is una before a consonant and un' before a vowel. The apostrophe is feminine only.

items.hr: un libro
items.en: a book
items.note: un is the default masculine form

items.hr: un amico
items.en: a male friend
items.note: still un before a vowel, and never with an apostrophe

items.hr: uno studente
items.en: a student
items.note: uno before s plus consonant, z, gn and ps

items.hr: una casa
items.en: a house
items.note: una before a feminine noun starting with a consonant

items.hr: un'amica
items.en: a female friend
items.note: the apostrophe shows that una has lost its a

activities.title: Un caffè, per favore
activities.intro: Choose the indefinite article and decide whether an apostrophe belongs there.

questions.prompt: Which spelling is correct for a male friend?
questions.explanation: Masculine un never takes an apostrophe, because nothing has been dropped from it.

questions.prompt: Which of these nouns needs the article uno?
questions.explanation: Nouns beginning with z take uno, just as they take lo with the definite article.

questions.prompt: Complete the sentence: Ho ___ sorella e due fratelli.
questions.explanation: Sorella is feminine and begins with a consonant, so it takes una.

questions.prompt: Complete the sentence: Marco ha ___ zaino nuovo.
questions.explanation: Zaino begins with z, which forces uno.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I have a female friend in Rome.'
questions.explanation: Una becomes un' before the vowel of amica, and a Roma needs no article.

activities.title: Ordinare al bar
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You order breakfast, and every item you name needs the right indefinite article.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Good morning! A coffee?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, un caffè e una brioche, grazie.
lines.en: Yes, a coffee and a pastry, thank you.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Here you are. Do you have a bag?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: No, I have a backpack.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: All right. And a water?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, a still water, thank you.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: That is three euros.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Here you are, thank you!

days.title: I numeri da 0 a 100
days.objective: Say and write every number from zero to one hundred, including the forms that drop a vowel or take an accent.
days.paretoFocus: Numbers carry prices, ages, addresses, phone numbers and times, so they are the first thing you must catch at full speed in a real shop.
drills: Count aloud from 0 to 20 twice, then count in tens up to 100.
Say your age, your house number and your phone number out loud in Italian.
Write out in words: 21, 28, 33, 47, 100.

items: What happens to venti and trenta before uno and otto?
Which numbers in the twenties and thirties carry a written accent, and where?
Say aloud: fifteen, sixty, eighty three, one hundred.

activities.title: Contare fino a cento
activities.intro: Numbers 11 to 16 end in -dici, and from 17 the order flips: diciassette, diciotto, diciannove. The tens drop their final vowel before uno and otto, so you get ventuno and ventotto. Compounds with tre take an accent, as in ventitré.

items.hr: zero, uno, due, tre, quattro, cinque
items.note: uno also works as the indefinite article

items.hr: sei, sette, otto, nove, dieci
items.note: sette and otto keep their double letters

items.hr: undici, dodici, tredici, quattordici, quindici
items.note: these all end in -dici

items.hr: venti, ventuno, ventitré, ventotto, trenta
items.note: venti loses its i before uno and otto, and tre takes an accent in compounds

items.hr: quaranta, cinquanta, sessanta, settanta, ottanta, novanta, cento
items.note: cento never changes its ending

activities.title: Numeri e prezzi
activities.intro: Write and recognise numbers, including the ones that lose a vowel.

questions.prompt: How is 21 written in Italian?
questions.explanation: Venti drops its final i before uno, and the two words are written as one.

questions.prompt: Which number does sessanta stand for?
questions.explanation: Sessanta is 60; 16 is sedici.

questions.prompt: Type the Italian word for the number 15.
questions.explanation: It belongs to the group ending in -dici, which runs from 11 to 16.

questions.prompt: Type the Italian word for the number 100.
questions.explanation: Cento is invariable and needs no article in front of it.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I have twenty euros.'
questions.explanation: Euro stays in the singular form after a number in everyday Italian.

activities.title: Al mercato
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You buy fruit and bread, so prices and quantities fly past in numbers.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Good morning! Two kilos of apples?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, two kilos, thank you.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: That is four euros fifty.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Here are five euros.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Thank you. Here are fifty cents.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Perfetto. E il pane? Tre euro?
lines.en: Perfect. And the bread? Three euros?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: No, two euros twenty.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: All right, thank you!

days.title: I giorni, i mesi e le stagioni
days.objective: Name the days, months and seasons, and say when something happens repeatedly or in a given month.
days.paretoFocus: Every appointment, opening time and travel plan runs on these words, and the small article in front of a day changes a single occasion into a weekly habit.
drills: Say the seven days aloud in order, then say them again starting from Thursday.
Say the twelve months aloud, then say the month of your birthday.
Say one true sentence about something you do every week, using il plus a day.

items: Which day of the week is feminine, and which article does it take?
What is the difference in meaning between domenica and la domenica?
Name the four seasons in Italian with their articles.

activities.title: Giorni, mesi, stagioni
activities.intro: Days and months are written in lower case in Italian. Five days end in a stressed i with an accent, and domenica is the only feminine day. Putting il or la before a day turns it into a weekly habit.

items.hr: lunedì, martedì, mercoledì, giovedì, venerdì
items.en: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday
items.note: all masculine, and all with an accent on the final i

items.hr: sabato e domenica
items.en: Saturday and Sunday
items.note: domenica is the only feminine day, so la domenica

items.hr: gennaio, febbraio, marzo, aprile
items.en: January, February, March, April
items.note: months take no article: a marzo means in March

items.hr: Il museo è aperto il lunedì.
items.en: The museum is open on Mondays.
items.note: il plus a day means every week, not one single time

items.hr: la primavera, l'estate, l'autunno, l'inverno
items.en: spring, summer, autumn, winter
items.note: in primavera, in estate and so on for in that season

activities.title: Quando?
activities.intro: Practise the calendar words and the little article that makes them habitual.

questions.prompt: Which day of the week is feminine in Italian?
questions.explanation: Domenica ends in -a and is feminine, so it takes la; the others are masculine.

questions.prompt: Which season does l'autunno name?
questions.explanation: Autunno is autumn; spring is la primavera.

questions.prompt: Type the Italian name of the day that falls between martedì and giovedì.
questions.explanation: The accent on the final i is obligatory in writing.

questions.prompt: Type the Italian name of the second month of the year.
questions.explanation: Note the double b, and remember that months are written in lower case.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'Sunday is a quiet day.'
questions.explanation: Tranquillo is masculine to agree with giorno, not with domenica.

activities.title: Il corso di italiano
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a friend fix which days and months things happen on.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ciao! Il corso è il lunedì?
lines.en: Hi! Is the course on Mondays?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: No, il corso è il martedì e il giovedì.
lines.en: No, the course is on Tuesdays and Thursdays.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: And in spring?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: In primavera il corso è il sabato.
lines.en: In spring the course is on Saturdays.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Are you free on Sunday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, I am free on Sundays.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bene. A settembre siamo a Roma.
lines.en: Good. In September we are in Rome.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Perfetto, settembre è un mese bellissimo.
lines.en: Perfect, September is a wonderful month.

days.title: L'ora
days.objective: Ask for the time and answer with full and half hours, quarters and minutes, including the special singular forms.
days.paretoFocus: Times are said dozens of times a day, and the singular forms for one o'clock, midday and midnight are the ones learners always get wrong.
drills: Say aloud the time on your clock now, then the same time one hour later.
Say these six times in Italian: 1:00, 3:15, 6:30, 8:45, 12:00, 24:00.
Ask the question for the time out loud five times until it comes without thinking.

items: Why do most times use sono le, and which three times use è instead?
How do you express a quarter past and a quarter to?
Say the current time in Italian, then say midday and midnight.

activities.title: Che ore sono?
activities.intro: The hours are counted as feminine plurals, because the word ore is understood, so you say sono le tre. One o'clock, midday and midnight are singular and take è. Minutes are added with e, and taken away from the next hour with meno.

items.hr: Che ore sono? Sono le tre.
items.en: What time is it? It is three o'clock.
items.note: sono le for every hour from two to twelve

items.hr: È l'una.
items.en: It is one o'clock.
items.note: singular, because one single hour is meant

items.hr: Sono le otto e mezza.
items.en: It is half past eight.
items.note: e mezza for half past, e un quarto for quarter past

items.hr: Sono le nove meno un quarto.
items.en: It is a quarter to nine.
items.note: meno subtracts minutes from the hour that is coming

items.hr: È mezzogiorno.
items.en: It is midday.
items.note: mezzogiorno and mezzanotte are singular and take è

activities.title: Dire l'ora
activities.intro: Give the time in Italian and choose between the singular and the plural form.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'It is one o'clock' in Italian?
questions.explanation: A single hour takes the singular è, and una becomes l'una before the verb drops the noun ora.

questions.prompt: What does 'Sono le sette e un quarto' mean?
options: It is a quarter to seven.
questions.explanation: E adds minutes to the hour already reached, so a quarter is added to seven.

questions.prompt: Complete the time for 10:30: Sono le dieci e ___.
questions.explanation: Half past is expressed with e mezza, agreeing with the understood word ora.

questions.prompt: Complete with the right form of essere: ___ mezzanotte.
questions.explanation: Midnight counts as one single moment, so the verb is singular and carries the accent.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'It is three o'clock.'
questions.explanation: Hours from two onwards are plural, so the verb is sono and the article is le.

activities.title: Siamo in orario
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You check the time and confirm an appointment, using both the plural and the singular forms.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Excuse me, what time is it?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sono le due e un quarto.
lines.en: It is a quarter past two.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Thank you. And the appointment? Three o'clock?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: No, half past three.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Good, we are on time.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes. It is still early now.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: And are you free at midday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, I am free at midday.

days.title: I pronomi personali soggetto
days.objective: Name all the subject pronouns, understand why Italians usually leave them out, and use the polite Lei correctly.
days.paretoFocus: Knowing when to drop the pronoun is what makes your Italian sound native instead of translated, and the polite Lei decides how strangers judge your manners.
drills: Say the seven subject pronouns aloud in order, twice.
Take three sentences you already know and say them once with the pronoun and once without it.
Say the same question twice, once to a friend with tu and once to a stranger with Lei.

items: Why can Italian leave the subject pronoun out when English cannot?
Which pronoun is the polite you, and does it change for a man and a woman?
Give the Italian pronouns for he, she, we, you plural and they.

activities.title: Io, tu, lui, lei
activities.intro: The subject pronouns are io, tu, lui, lei, noi, voi and loro. Because every verb ending already shows the person, the pronoun is normally dropped, and it is used mainly for contrast or emphasis. The polite you is Lei, written with a capital, and it takes the same verb form as lei.

items.hr: Io sono Marco e tu sei Anna.
items.en: I am Marco and you are Anna.
items.note: the pronouns are kept here because the two people are contrasted

items.hr: Sono italiano.
items.en: I am Italian.
items.note: no pronoun needed, since sono can only belong to io here

items.hr: Lui è a Roma, lei è a Milano.
items.en: He is in Rome, she is in Milan.
items.note: lui for he, lei for she

items.hr: Noi siamo qui, voi siete lì.
items.en: We are here, you are there.
items.note: voi is you when speaking to more than one person

items.hr: Lei è italiano, signor Rossi?
items.en: Are you Italian, Mr Rossi?
items.note: polite Lei takes a third person verb, but the adjective matches the real person

activities.title: Chi parla?
activities.intro: Identify and supply the right subject pronoun, formal or informal.

questions.prompt: Which pronoun means they?
questions.explanation: Loro covers they for any mix of genders.

questions.prompt: Why does Italian often leave out the subject pronoun?
options: because the verb ending already shows the person
because it is only used in writing
questions.answer: because the verb ending already shows the person
questions.explanation: Each person has its own ending, so the pronoun would only repeat information.

questions.prompt: Add the pronoun meaning we: ___ siamo studenti.
questions.explanation: The verb siamo belongs to the first person plural.

questions.prompt: Add the polite pronoun for you, addressing Mrs Bianchi: ___ è la direttrice?
questions.explanation: The polite form is written with a capital and takes a third person verb.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'He is here and she is there.'
questions.explanation: Both pronouns are kept because the sentence contrasts two people.

activities.title: In ufficio
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A polite work introduction where the pronouns are kept for contrast and for formality.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Buongiorno. Lei è il signor Ferrari?
lines.en: Good morning. Are you Mr Ferrari?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, that is me. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: I am Anna Conti.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Nice to meet you. Are you from Milan?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: No, noi siamo di Bologna. Loro sono di Milano.
lines.en: No, we are from Bologna. They are from Milan.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: And is he a colleague?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sì, lui è un collega e lei è la direttrice.
lines.en: Yes, he is a colleague and she is the director.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Nice to meet you. Goodbye!

days.title: Il presente dei verbi in -are
days.objective: Conjugate regular -are verbs in all six persons of the present tense and use them to talk about daily life.
days.paretoFocus: About seventy per cent of Italian verbs end in -are, so one set of six endings unlocks the largest group of verbs in the language.
drills: Conjugate parlare aloud through all six persons, then do the same with lavorare.
Say five true sentences about your day using different -are verbs.
Say the loro form of three verbs aloud and put the stress on the first syllable each time.

items: What are the six present tense endings for -are verbs?
Why is the noi form of mangiare spelled with only one i?
Say in Italian: I speak Italian, we work here, they live in Rome.

activities.title: Parlare, lavorare, abitare
activities.intro: To conjugate a regular -are verb, remove the -are and add -o, -i, -a, -iamo, -ate, -ano. The loro form is stressed on the first syllable, not on the ending. Verbs whose stem ends in -i, like mangiare, never double that i.

items.hr: Io parlo italiano.
items.en: I speak Italian.
items.note: parlare minus -are plus -o

items.hr: Tu parli bene.
items.en: You speak well.
items.note: the tu ending is -i

items.hr: Anna lavora a Roma.
items.en: Anna works in Rome.
items.note: the lui and lei ending is -a

items.hr: Noi mangiamo la pizza.
items.en: We eat the pizza.
items.note: mangiamo keeps one i only, never mangiiamo

items.hr: Voi studiate e loro abitano qui.
items.en: You study and they live here.
items.note: -ate for voi, -ano for loro with the stress on the first syllable

activities.title: Le desinenze in -are
activities.intro: Attach the right -are ending to each subject.

questions.prompt: Complete correctly: Loro ___ in centro.
questions.explanation: The loro ending for -are verbs is -ano, and the stress falls on the first syllable.

questions.prompt: Which ending goes with noi for a regular -are verb?
questions.explanation: Noi takes -iamo, and this same ending appears in all three verb families.

questions.prompt: Complete with parlare: Tu ___ francese?
questions.explanation: The tu ending for -are verbs is -i.

questions.prompt: Complete with lavorare: Noi ___ in un ristorante.
questions.explanation: Remove -are from lavorare and add -iamo.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I speak a little Italian.'
questions.explanation: Un po' di is a fixed quantity phrase, and po' keeps its apostrophe.

activities.title: Studio e lavoro
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You talk about work and study, so every line contains a regular -are verb.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Hi! Do you speak Italian?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, parlo un po' di italiano.
lines.en: Yes, I speak a little Italian.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Good! Do you work in Rome?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, I work in an office.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: And do you study as well?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, I study in the evening.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Shall we eat together tomorrow?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Gladly! Let us eat a pizza.

days.title: Il presente dei verbi in -ere
days.objective: Conjugate regular -ere verbs in the present tense and hear where their endings differ from the -are group.
days.paretoFocus: The -ere family contains many of the highest frequency actions in daily life, such as taking, reading, writing and seeing, so it doubles what you can say.
drills: Conjugate prendere aloud through all six persons, then leggere.
Say three sentences with -are verbs and three with -ere verbs, listening for the third person -a against -e.
Write the loro form of four -ere verbs and check that each one ends in -ono.

items: What are the six present tense endings for -ere verbs?
Which two persons have exactly the same ending in both the -are and the -ere family?
Say in Italian: I have a coffee, she reads a book, they write a message.

activities.title: Prendere, leggere, scrivere
activities.intro: Remove -ere and add -o, -i, -e, -iamo, -ete, -ono. Only the io, tu and noi endings are shared with the -are family, so the third person and the loro form are where mistakes happen. Prendere is also the verb Italians use for ordering food and drink.

items.hr: Io prendo un caffè.
items.en: I have a coffee.
items.note: prendere is used for ordering, not mangiare or bere

items.hr: Tu leggi un libro.
items.en: You are reading a book.
items.note: the present covers both I read and I am reading

items.hr: Marco scrive una lettera.
items.en: Marco writes a letter.
items.note: the ending is -e here, where an -are verb would have -a

items.hr: Noi vediamo la città.
items.en: We see the city.
items.note: -iamo is shared with the -are verbs; città keeps its accent

items.hr: Voi chiedete e loro rispondono.
items.en: You ask and they answer.
items.note: -ete for voi and -ono for loro

activities.title: Le desinenze in -ere
activities.intro: Choose the -ere ending that matches each subject.

questions.prompt: Complete correctly: Lei ___ un libro ogni settimana.
questions.explanation: The third person singular of an -ere verb ends in -e, giving legge with its double g.

questions.prompt: Which ending do -ere verbs take with loro?
questions.explanation: Verbs in -are take -ano while verbs in -ere take -ono, which is a very common exam trap.

questions.prompt: Complete with prendere: Voi ___ il treno?
questions.explanation: The voi ending for -ere verbs is -ete.

questions.prompt: Complete with scrivere: Io ___ una mail.
questions.explanation: The io ending is -o for all three verb families.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'They read the newspaper.'
questions.explanation: The loro form leggono already shows the subject, so no pronoun is needed.

activities.title: Colazione al bar
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You order and chat over breakfast, using -ere verbs throughout.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Are you having a coffee?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, prendo un caffè e un cornetto.
lines.en: Yes, I am having a coffee and a croissant.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Is Anna having a coffee too?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: No, she is having a tea.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Do you read the newspaper here?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, leggiamo il giornale ogni mattina.
lines.en: Yes, we read the newspaper every morning.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: And the boys? Are they writing messages?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, they are always writing messages.

days.title: Il presente dei verbi in -ire
days.objective: Conjugate both kinds of -ire verb, the plain type like dormire and the -isc- type like capire.
days.paretoFocus: Capire and finire are among the verbs a beginner needs most, and they belong to the -isc- group, so ignoring that pattern breaks your most useful sentences.
drills: Conjugate dormire aloud through all six persons, then capire, and notice where -isc- appears.
Say four sentences with capire, changing the person each time.
List the two persons that never take -isc- and say their forms out loud.

items: Which four persons of capire take -isc-, and which two do not?
Are the endings of dormire and capire the same once -isc- is removed?
Say in Italian: I sleep eight hours, we finish early, they understand everything.

activities.title: Dormire e capire
activities.intro: Regular -ire verbs take -o, -i, -e, -iamo, -ite, -ono. A large second group inserts -isc- between the stem and the ending in the three singular persons and in loro, but never in noi and voi. The endings themselves are identical in both groups.

items.hr: Io dormo otto ore.
items.en: I sleep eight hours.
items.note: dormire belongs to the plain group

items.hr: Tu parti domani.
items.en: You are leaving tomorrow.
items.note: partire is plain too, and the present can express a near future

items.hr: Marco apre la finestra.
items.en: Marco opens the window.
items.note: the third person ending is -e, as with -ere verbs

items.hr: Io capisco, tu capisci, lui capisce.
items.en: I understand, you understand, he understands.
items.note: capire inserts -isc- in all three singular persons

items.hr: Noi finiamo e loro finiscono.
items.en: We finish and they finish.
items.note: noi and voi have no -isc-: finiamo, finite; loro has it

activities.title: Con o senza isc
activities.intro: Decide whether the form needs -isc- and add the right ending.

questions.prompt: Complete correctly: Loro ___ il lavoro oggi.
questions.explanation: Loro belongs to the group of persons that keep -isc-, giving finiscono.

questions.prompt: Which of these verbs adds -isc- in the present tense?
questions.explanation: Capire and finire follow the -isc- pattern; the other three are plain.

questions.prompt: Complete with dormire: Voi ___ molto.
questions.explanation: The voi ending for -ire verbs is -ite, and voi never takes -isc-.

questions.prompt: Complete with capire: Io ___ bene l'italiano.
questions.explanation: The io form inserts -isc- before the ending -o.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'We finish at midday.'
questions.explanation: Noi drops the -isc-, so the form is simply finiamo.

activities.title: Partenze
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You talk about travel plans and about what you follow or miss, so dormire and capire keep taking turns.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Do you understand Italian?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, I understand almost everything.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Good. Are you leaving tomorrow?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, I am leaving tomorrow morning.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E i ragazzi partono con te?
lines.en: And are the boys leaving with you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: No, they are leaving on Sunday.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Are you finishing early today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, finiamo presto e dormiamo un po'.
lines.en: Yes, we finish early and sleep a little.

days.title: I verbi irregolari andare, fare, stare, dare
days.objective: Use the present tense of andare, fare, stare and dare, four irregular verbs that appear constantly in everyday speech.
days.paretoFocus: These four verbs cover going somewhere, doing something, feeling well and giving, which is most of what small talk is made of.
drills: Conjugate all four verbs aloud, one after another, and notice that noi is regular every time.
Say where you are going today, what you are doing and how you are feeling.
Write the third person singular of dare and mark the accent on it.

items: Which two forms of fare keep a double c, and which persons are they?
Which form of dare carries a written accent, and why is it needed?
Say in Italian: I am well, we are going to Rome, they are giving a party.

activities.title: Vado, faccio, sto, do
activities.intro: These four verbs are irregular but they share a shape: a very short io form and a loro form ending in -nno. The noi form stays regular with -iamo in all four. Stare covers how you feel, while essere covers who you are.

items.hr: Io vado a Roma.
items.en: I am going to Rome.
items.note: andare: vado, vai, va, andiamo, andate, vanno

items.hr: Tu fai colazione?
items.en: Do you have breakfast?
items.note: fare: faccio, fai, fa, facciamo, fate, fanno

items.hr: Come stai? Sto bene.
items.en: How are you? I am well.
items.note: stare: sto, stai, sta, stiamo, state, stanno

items.hr: Loro danno una festa.
items.en: They are giving a party.
items.note: dare: do, dai, dà, diamo, date, danno

items.hr: Noi andiamo a scuola e facciamo i compiti.
items.en: We go to school and do the homework.
items.note: the noi form keeps the regular -iamo in every one of the four

activities.title: Quattro verbi irregolari
activities.intro: Produce the irregular forms and watch the written accent.

questions.prompt: Complete correctly: Loro ___ in centro ogni sabato.
questions.explanation: The loro form of andare is vanno, with the typical double n of this small group.

questions.prompt: Which form of dare carries a written accent?
questions.explanation: The accent separates the verb dà from the preposition da, which has no accent.

questions.prompt: Complete with fare: Io ___ colazione a casa.
questions.explanation: The io form of fare is faccio, with a double c.

questions.prompt: Ask a woman politely how she is feeling, filling the gap: Come ___, signora?
questions.explanation: The verb is stare, and the polite form borrows the third person singular.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'Tomorrow we are going to Rome.'
questions.explanation: Andare takes a before a city name, and the time word can open the sentence.

activities.title: Una passeggiata in centro
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend asks how you are and what you are doing, so all four irregular verbs appear.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Hi! How are you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: I am well, thank you. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sto benissimo. Vai in centro oggi?
lines.en: I am very well. Are you going to the centre today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, vado in centro con Anna.
lines.en: Yes, I am going to the centre with Anna.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Are you going on foot?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, andiamo a piedi e facciamo una passeggiata.
lines.en: Yes, we are going on foot and taking a walk.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bene. Domani i ragazzi danno una festa.
lines.en: Good. Tomorrow the boys are giving a party.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Perfect, we are going too.

days.title: La negazione con non
days.objective: Make any sentence negative by putting non directly before the verb, and use non with mai and niente.
days.paretoFocus: Negation is needed in half of all real conversations, and Italian does it with one small word and no auxiliary, which is far simpler than English once you trust it.
drills: Take five affirmative sentences you can already say and make each one negative.
Say three things you never do, using non and mai.
Answer a yes or no question out loud twice, once positively and once negatively.

items: Where exactly does non go in the sentence?
Why is 'Non mangio mai la carne' correct even though it contains two negative words?
Say in Italian: I am not hungry, Anna is not at home, we never go there.

activities.title: Dire di no
activities.intro: To make a sentence negative, put non immediately before the verb. Italian needs no extra helping word, so there is nothing like do or does. When mai or niente is used, non stays in place as well, and this double negative is the correct form.

items.hr: Non parlo tedesco.
items.en: I do not speak German.
items.note: non sits directly in front of the verb, and nothing is added

items.hr: Marco non è a casa.
items.en: Marco is not at home.
items.note: non comes before è, never after it

items.hr: Non ho fame, grazie.
items.en: I am not hungry, thank you.
items.note: the subject pronoun is dropped, so non opens the sentence

items.hr: Non capisco niente.
items.en: I do not understand anything.
items.note: both non and niente are required in Italian

items.hr: Non vado mai a teatro.
items.en: I never go to the theatre.
items.note: non before the verb, mai after it

activities.title: Frasi negative
activities.intro: Place the negative words correctly inside the sentence.

questions.prompt: Which sentence puts the negative word in the right place?
questions.explanation: Non goes between the subject and the verb; no on its own is only the answer word.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I never eat meat' in Italian?
questions.explanation: Non stays before the verb and mai follows it; both words are needed.

questions.prompt: Add the missing negative word: Anna ___ sta bene oggi.
questions.explanation: The negative word goes directly in front of the conjugated verb.

questions.prompt: Complete so that the sentence means 'I have nothing': Non ho ___.
questions.explanation: Niente, or the more formal nulla, follows the verb while non stays before it.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'We are not tired.'
questions.explanation: Non comes before the verb siamo, and stanchi agrees with the plural subject.

activities.title: No, grazie
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You politely turn things down, so almost every answer is negative.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Are you having an ice cream?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: No, thank you, I am not having anything.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Are you not hungry?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: No, non ho fame. E tu?
lines.en: No, I am not hungry. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Io non mangio mai la sera.
lines.en: I never eat in the evening.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Anche Anna non mangia la sera?
lines.en: Does Anna not eat in the evening either?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: No, lei non è qui oggi.
lines.en: No, she is not here today.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: All right, it does not matter.

days.title: Le domande e le parole interrogative
days.objective: Ask yes or no questions with intonation alone, and use the main question words to ask for specific information.
days.paretoFocus: Asking questions is how you keep a conversation alive when your vocabulary is small, and it turns any native speaker into your free teacher.
drills: Say the same sentence twice, once as a statement and once as a question, changing only your voice.
Write one question with each of these words: come, dove, quando, chi, quanto.
Ask five questions out loud about a person you know, and answer them yourself.

items: How is a yes or no question formed in Italian, compared with English?
Which single Italian word covers both why and because?
Ask in Italian: where do you live, what are you doing, how much does it cost?

activities.title: Fare domande
activities.intro: A yes or no question in Italian uses exactly the same word order as a statement; only the rising voice changes. For specific information, put the question word at the front. Perché serves as both why and because, so it can open a question and its answer.

items.hr: Parli italiano?
items.en: Do you speak Italian?
items.note: same words as the statement, but with a rising voice

items.hr: Come ti chiami? Dove abiti?
items.en: What is your name? Where do you live?
items.note: come means how, dove means where

items.hr: Che cosa fai? Chi è?
items.en: What are you doing? Who is it?
items.note: che cosa, or just cosa, means what; chi means who

items.hr: Quando parte il treno? Perché?
items.en: When does the train leave? Why?
items.note: after a question word the subject often follows the verb

items.hr: Quanto costa? Quale preferisci?
items.en: How much does it cost? Which one do you prefer?
items.note: quanto for how much, quale for which

activities.title: Parole interrogative
activities.intro: Match each question word to the information it asks for.

questions.prompt: Which word asks about a place?
questions.explanation: Dove asks where; quando asks when and chi asks who.

questions.prompt: The exchange 'Perché non mangi? Perché non ho fame.' shows that perché means:
questions.explanation: The same word opens the question and introduces the reason in the answer.

questions.prompt: Add the missing question word: ___ costa il biglietto?
questions.explanation: Prices are asked about with quanto, meaning how much.

questions.prompt: Add the missing question word: ___ è quella signora?
questions.explanation: Chi asks about a person's identity.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'When does the lesson finish?'
questions.explanation: The question word comes first and the subject follows the verb.

activities.title: Facciamo conoscenza
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Two people swap information, so almost every line is a question.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Hi! What is your name?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: My name is Luca. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: I am Sara. Where do you live?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Abito a Torino. Tu dove abiti?
lines.en: I live in Turin. Where do you live?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Abito a Napoli. Che cosa fai a Torino?
lines.en: I live in Naples. What do you do in Turin?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Studio e lavoro in un bar.
lines.en: I study and I work in a bar.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: When do you finish today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: I finish early, around six.

days.title: La famiglia
days.objective: Name the members of a family in Italian and describe who is in yours.
days.paretoFocus: Family is the first personal topic anyone asks about, and it is a fixed part of the speaking test at this level.
drills: Say aloud the members of your own family with their articles.
Say how many brothers, sisters and cousins you have.
Draw a quick family tree and label every person in Italian.

items: What does the word genitori cover, and is it masculine or feminine?
Why does zio take lo rather than il?
Say in Italian: the parents, the grandparents, the aunt, the female cousin.

activities.title: Parenti e famiglia
activities.intro: Family words come in masculine and feminine pairs, and the masculine plural covers a mixed group. So i figli means the children of a family whatever their genders, and i nonni means the grandparents together.

items.hr: il padre, la madre, i genitori
items.en: the father, the mother, the parents
items.note: genitori is the everyday word for both parents together

items.hr: il fratello, la sorella
items.en: the brother, the sister
items.note: Italian has no single everyday word for sibling

items.hr: il figlio, la figlia, i figli
items.en: the son, the daughter, the children
items.note: i figli means sons and daughters, not small children

items.hr: il nonno, la nonna, i nonni
items.en: the grandfather, the grandmother, the grandparents
items.note: remember the double n in all three

items.hr: lo zio, la zia, il cugino, la cugina
items.en: the uncle, the aunt, the male cousin, the female cousin
items.note: lo zio, because the word begins with z

activities.title: Chi è in famiglia
activities.intro: Name family members and give them the right article and number.

questions.prompt: What does the word genitori refer to?
questions.explanation: Genitori covers a mother and a father together; grandparents are i nonni.

questions.prompt: Which definite article belongs before the noun zia?
questions.explanation: Zia is feminine and begins with a consonant, so lo does not apply to it.

questions.prompt: Type the Italian for the grandparents, with its article.
questions.explanation: The masculine plural covers a grandmother and a grandfather together.

questions.prompt: Complete the sentence: Ho due ___ e una sorella.
questions.explanation: After a number the noun goes into the plural, so fratello becomes fratelli.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'The aunt has three children.'
questions.explanation: Zia takes la, and figli is the masculine plural covering sons and daughters.

activities.title: La mia famiglia
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Someone asks about your relatives, so you name several of them in a row.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Do you have any brothers or sisters?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, ho un fratello e due sorelle.
lines.en: Yes, I have one brother and two sisters.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E i genitori abitano a Roma?
lines.en: And do the parents live in Rome?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: No, i genitori abitano a Bari.
lines.en: No, the parents live in Bari.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Anche i nonni sono a Bari?
lines.en: Are the grandparents in Bari too?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, i nonni e una zia.
lines.en: Yes, the grandparents and an aunt.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: How many cousins do you have?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ho quattro cugini e una cugina.
lines.en: I have four male cousins and one female cousin.

days.title: Gli aggettivi e l'accordo
days.objective: Make every adjective agree with its noun in gender and number, including the adjectives that end in -e.
days.paretoFocus: Agreement runs through every description you will ever give, and a wrong ending is the mistake examiners notice first in both speaking and writing.
drills: Describe four objects around you, changing the adjective ending to match each noun.
Take one adjective in -o and say all four of its forms aloud.
Write two sentences with an adjective in -e and check that the singular form does not change with gender.

items: What are the four forms of an adjective like rosso?
How many singular forms does an adjective like grande have, and how many plural forms?
Say in Italian: a small house, small houses, an interesting book, interesting books.

activities.title: L'accordo degli aggettivi
activities.intro: Adjectives ending in -o have four forms: -o, -a, -i, -e. Adjectives ending in -e have only two: -e in the singular for both genders, and -i in the plural for both genders. The adjective normally follows the noun it describes.

items.hr: un ragazzo italiano, una ragazza italiana
items.en: an Italian boy, an Italian girl
items.note: the -o form becomes -a for a feminine noun

items.hr: i ragazzi italiani, le ragazze italiane
items.en: Italian boys, Italian girls
items.note: -i for the masculine plural, -e for the feminine plural

items.hr: un libro interessante, una storia interessante
items.en: an interesting book, an interesting story
items.note: one singular form serves both genders

items.hr: libri interessanti, storie interessanti
items.en: interesting books, interesting stories
items.note: and one plural form serves both genders too

items.hr: La casa è piccola ma bella.
items.en: The house is small but beautiful.
items.note: adjectives after essere still agree with the subject

activities.title: Concordanza
activities.intro: Give each adjective the ending its noun demands.

questions.prompt: Complete correctly: Le ragazze sono ___.
questions.explanation: The subject is feminine plural, and the h keeps the c hard before the -e ending.

questions.prompt: How many singular forms does an adjective like grande have?
questions.explanation: Adjectives in -e do not change for gender in the singular, only for number.

questions.prompt: Give the right form of rosso: Le macchine sono ___.
questions.explanation: Macchine is feminine plural, so the adjective takes -e.

questions.prompt: Give the right form of alto: I ragazzi sono ___.
questions.explanation: Ragazzi is masculine plural, so the adjective takes -i.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'The girls are Italian.'
questions.explanation: Feminine plural subject, so both the article and the adjective end in -e.

activities.title: Com'è la casa nuova?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You describe a new flat, and each adjective has to match the noun in front of it.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: What is the new house like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: It is small but very beautiful.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: And are the rooms big?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, le camere sono grandi e luminose.
lines.en: Yes, the rooms are big and bright.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Are the neighbours nice?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, they are nice and quiet.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: How lovely! And the area?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: È una zona tranquilla e verde.
lines.en: It is a quiet and green area.

days.title: Gli aggettivi possessivi
days.objective: Say what belongs to whom with mio, tuo, suo, nostro, vostro and loro, and apply the family exception.
days.paretoFocus: Possessives are unavoidable when talking about your things and your relatives, and Italian agrees them with the object owned, not with the owner, which reverses the English habit.
drills: Name five things you own, each with the right form of mio.
Say four sentences about single family members, leaving the article out each time.
Say the same four sentences again in the plural, putting the article back in.

items: Does the possessive agree with the owner or with the thing owned?
When is the article left out before a possessive?
Say in Italian: my friends, our school, their dog, your bag.

activities.title: Il mio, la mia, i miei
activities.intro: Italian possessives normally keep the definite article and agree with the thing owned, not with its owner. The one exception is a single family member, where the article disappears. Loro never changes its ending and always keeps the article.

items.hr: il mio libro, la mia casa
items.en: my book, my house
items.note: the article stays, and mio matches the noun that follows

items.hr: i miei amici, le mie amiche
items.en: my male friends, my female friends
items.note: the plural forms are irregular: i miei, le mie

items.hr: il tuo zaino, la tua borsa
items.en: your backpack, your bag
items.note: suo means his or her, agreeing with the object, not the owner

items.hr: mia madre e mio fratello
items.en: my mother and my brother
items.note: no article before a single family member in the singular

items.hr: la nostra scuola e il vostro professore
items.en: our school and your teacher
items.note: loro never changes: il loro cane, la loro casa

activities.title: Di chi è?
activities.intro: Choose the possessive form and decide whether the article belongs there.

questions.prompt: Which phrase for my sister is correct?
questions.explanation: A single family member in the singular drops the article before the possessive.

questions.prompt: Complete correctly: ___ amici arrivano oggi.
questions.explanation: Amici is masculine plural, so the possessive and its article both go into the masculine plural.

questions.prompt: Add the possessive meaning our: ___ casa è a Verona.
questions.explanation: Casa is feminine singular, so the article and the possessive both take the feminine form.

questions.prompt: Add the possessive meaning their: Il ___ cane è piccolo.
questions.explanation: Loro is invariable, so only the article shows gender and number.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'My brother works in Milan.'
questions.explanation: Fratello is a single family member, so the article is dropped in front of mio.

activities.title: Le nostre cose
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You sort out whose belongings are whose, and mention relatives along the way.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ciao! Questa è la tua borsa?
lines.en: Hi! Is this your bag?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: No, la mia borsa è nera. Questa è di Anna.
lines.en: No, my bag is black. This one is Anna's.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E i tuoi amici? Sono qui?
lines.en: And your friends? Are they here?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, i miei amici sono in giardino.
lines.en: Yes, my friends are in the garden.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Is your sister arriving today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, mia sorella arriva con i nostri genitori.
lines.en: Yes, my sister is arriving with our parents.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: And their dog?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Il loro cane resta a casa.
lines.en: Their dog is staying at home.

days.title: I dimostrativi: questo e quello
days.objective: Point at and talk about things near you and far from you using every form of questo and quello, both before a noun and on its own.
days.paretoFocus: You cannot shop, order, choose or ask for anything concrete without saying 'this one' and 'that one', and quello reuses the article rules you already know, so one lesson unlocks a whole daily-use system.
drills: Walk around your home and name ten objects twice: once with questo/questa/questi/queste, once with the matching quello form.
Write five pairs of sentences contrasting near and far, for example one about a book on your desk and one about a book across the room.
Say five short answers that use quello as a pronoun with no noun after it, such as choosing between two coffees.

items: Which four forms does questo have, and what do they agree with?
Which quello form goes before a masculine noun starting with s plus consonant or z, and why?
How do you say 'This one is mine, that one is not' without naming the object?

activities.title: Questo qui, quello là
activities.intro: Questo means 'this' (near me) and quello means 'that' (further away), and both agree with the noun in gender and number. Questo has only four forms: questo, questa, questi, queste. Quello changes shape exactly like the definite article you already know, so il gives quel, lo gives quello, l' gives quell', la gives quella, i gives quei, gli gives quegli and le gives quelle.

items.hr: Questo libro è mio, quel libro è di Marco.
items.en: This book is mine, that book is Marco's.
items.note: il libro -> quel libro

items.hr: Questa casa è piccola, ma quella casa è enorme.
items.en: This house is small, but that house is enormous.
items.note: la casa -> quella casa

items.hr: Quello zaino è pesante e quell'armadio è troppo grande.
items.en: That backpack is heavy and that wardrobe is too big.
items.note: lo zaino -> quello zaino; l'armadio -> quell'armadio

items.hr: Quei ragazzi sono italiani, quegli studenti invece sono spagnoli.
items.en: Those boys are Italian, those students on the other hand are Spanish.
