items.note: i ragazzi -> quei ragazzi; gli studenti -> quegli studenti

items.hr: Questo è mio, quello no.
items.en: This one is mine, that one is not.
items.note: with no noun after it, quello keeps the simple forms: quello, quella, quelli, quelle

activities.title: Vicino o lontano?
activities.intro: Choose the right demonstrative form and check your agreement.

questions.prompt: Completa: ___ zaino in fondo alla stanza è di Luca.
questions.explanation: Zaino starts with s plus consonant, so it takes lo zaino. Quello follows the same sound rule as the article, giving quello zaino.

questions.prompt: Completa con l'aggettivo giusto: Queste scarpe sono molto ___.
questions.explanation: Scarpe is feminine plural, so the adjective ends in -e: comode. Adjectives agree with the noun exactly as demonstratives do.

questions.prompt: Completa: ___ amici laggiù sono di Roma.
questions.explanation: Amici takes gli amici because it starts with a vowel, and the matching far form is quegli.

questions.prompt: Completa con la forma di vicinanza: ___ ragazza seduta accanto a me è mia sorella.
questions.explanation: Ragazza is feminine singular and the person is right next to the speaker, so you need the near form questa.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'This one is mine, that one is not.'
questions.explanation: Used alone as pronouns, questo and quello keep their simple forms and need no noun after them.

activities.title: In negozio
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are choosing clothes in a shop and you keep contrasting what is near you with what is further away.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Good morning, are you looking for anything in particular?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, mi piace questa giacca, ma vorrei vedere anche quella in vetrina.
lines.en: Yes, I like this jacket, but I would also like to see that one in the window.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Quella nera con i bottoni grandi? È molto elegante.
lines.en: That black one with the big buttons? It is very elegant.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Esatto, quella. Questi pantaloni invece sono troppo stretti.
lines.en: Exactly, that one. These trousers on the other hand are too tight.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Allora provi quegli altri, sono più comodi.
lines.en: Then try those other ones, they are more comfortable.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Va bene. Prendo questa giacca e quei pantaloni grigi.
lines.en: All right. I will take this jacket and those grey trousers.

days.title: Gli indefiniti e le frasi esclamative
days.objective: Talk about vague or unspecified quantities with ogni, qualche, alcuni, qualcosa, qualcuno, ognuno and tutti, and react to things with Che, Quanto and Come.
days.paretoFocus: Real conversation is full of 'every day', 'some friends', 'someone', 'something' and spontaneous reactions like 'How nice!', and getting the singular trap of ogni and qualche right stops a mistake that lasts for years.
drills: Write five sentences about your week that all begin with Ogni, checking that the noun after it is singular every time.
Rewrite three of those sentences with qualche, keeping the noun singular even when you mean more than one.
React out loud to five photos or things around you using Che, Quanto and Come.

items: What number does the noun after ogni and qualche always take?
Which two indefinites mean 'some' with a plural noun, and how do they differ?
Give one exclamation with Che plus a noun and one with Come plus a verb.

activities.title: Ogni giorno, che bello!
activities.intro: Ogni and qualche are always followed by a SINGULAR noun, even when the meaning is plural: qualche amico means 'a few friends'. For a plural noun you use alcuni or alcune instead. To react to something you use Che plus a noun or adjective, Quanto è plus an adjective, or Come plus a verb.

items.hr: Ogni giorno studio un po' di italiano.
items.en: Every day I study a bit of Italian.
items.note: ogni + singular noun always: ogni giorno, never ogni giorni

items.hr: Ho qualche domanda per te.
items.en: I have a few questions for you.
items.note: qualche + singular noun, plural meaning: qualche domanda = some questions

items.hr: Alcuni studenti sono in ritardo, alcune ragazze arrivano adesso.
items.en: Some students are late, some girls are arriving now.
items.note: alcuni / alcune + plural noun

items.hr: Qualcuno vuole qualcosa da bere? Ognuno paga il suo caffè e tutti sono contenti.
items.en: Does someone want something to drink? Everyone pays for their own coffee and everybody is happy.
items.note: qualcuno, qualcosa and ognuno are always singular; tutti is plural

items.hr: Che bella giornata! Quanto è caro questo vestito! Come sei gentile!
items.en: What a beautiful day! How expensive this dress is! How kind you are!
items.note: Che + noun or adjective, Quanto è + adjective, Come + verb

activities.title: Qualcosa per tutti
activities.intro: Practise the indefinites and the exclamations.

questions.prompt: Quale frase è corretta?
questions.explanation: Qualche is always followed by a singular noun, so amico stays singular even though the meaning is 'a few friends'.

questions.prompt: Scegli l'articolo corretto: ___ zaino nuovo.
questions.explanation: Masculine nouns starting with s plus consonant take lo, so lo zaino. The same sound rule shapes many other words that come before the noun.

questions.prompt: Completa l'esclamazione davanti a un panorama bellissimo: ___ bello!
questions.explanation: The simplest Italian exclamation puts this short word before an adjective or a noun.

questions.prompt: Completa: ___ mattina mi alzo alle sette.
questions.explanation: For a repeated daily habit you need the indefinite that means 'every', and it takes the singular noun mattina.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'Someone wants something to drink.'
questions.explanation: Qualcuno and qualcosa are invariable, and qualcosa da plus infinitive is the fixed way to say 'something to drink or eat'.

activities.title: Programmi per il sabato
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend suggests going out and you both use indefinites and exclamations naturally.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Che bella giornata! Hai qualche programma per oggi?
lines.en: What a beautiful day! Do you have any plans for today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, ogni sabato vado al mercato. Vuoi venire?
lines.en: Yes, every Saturday I go to the market. Do you want to come?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Volentieri, al mercato c'è sempre qualcosa di interessante.
lines.en: Gladly, at the market there is always something interesting.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Vero, e alcuni banchi hanno una frutta buonissima.
lines.en: True, and some stalls have really excellent fruit.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Quanto è caro il pesce però!
lines.en: How expensive the fish is though!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: È vero, ma non tutto è caro: qualcuno vende ancora a poco.
lines.en: That is true, but not everything is expensive: some people still sell cheaply.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Come sei ottimista! Andiamo, ognuno compra quello che vuole.
lines.en: How optimistic you are! Let's go, everyone buys what they want.

days.title: Le preposizioni semplici
days.objective: Use the simple prepositions di, a, da, in, con, su, per and tra to say where you are from, where you are going and where things are.
days.paretoFocus: Almost every sentence about places, times and people needs one of these eight small words, and choosing the wrong one is the fastest way to sound foreign.
drills: Say out loud your origin, your home city and today's destination, using a different preposition in each sentence.
Look at five objects around you and say where each one is, using su or in.
Write four sentences about a trip: the city you leave from, the city you go to, who you travel with and what the trip is for.

items: Which preposition goes before a city name, and which one goes before a country name?
Say 'I come back from work at seven' in Italian.
Give two meanings of di and one example sentence for each.

activities.title: Di, a, da, in, su
activities.intro: Italian has eight of these one-word prepositions, tra also being written fra. Di shows origin and possession, a shows destination and clock time, da shows the point you come from, in shows countries and enclosed spaces, su means on, and per, con and tra cover purpose, company and position between. Learn each one with a whole example sentence, because the choice is fixed by the following word, not by logic.

items.hr: Sono di Roma, ma adesso lavoro a Bologna.
items.en: I am from Rome, but now I work in Bologna.
items.note: di for origin; a before city names

items.hr: Domani vado a Firenze con mia sorella.
items.en: Tomorrow I am going to Florence with my sister.
items.note: a = destination, con = with

items.hr: Torno da scuola alle quattro.
items.en: I come back from school at four.
items.note: da = from a place or person

items.hr: Abito in Italia e studio in biblioteca.
items.en: I live in Italy and I study in the library.
items.note: in before countries and closed spaces

items.hr: Il gatto dorme su una sedia.
items.en: The cat sleeps on a chair.
items.note: su = on

activities.title: Scegli la preposizione
activities.intro: Practise picking the right simple preposition for places, origins and destinations.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Marta è ___ Firenze.' (Marta is from Florence.)
questions.explanation: Origin with the verb essere uses di: sono di Roma, e di Napoli.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Sabato andiamo ___ Napoli.' (On Saturday we are going to Naples.)
questions.explanation: Cities take a for destination; countries and regions take in (vado a Napoli, vado in Sicilia).

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Torno ___ Roma stasera.' (I am coming back from Rome tonight.)
questions.explanation: Da marks the place you are coming away from.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Le chiavi sono ___ un libro.' (The keys are on a book.)
questions.explanation: Su means on, resting on a surface.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I am going to Rome with my sister.'
questions.explanation: Destination city takes a, and con introduces the person you are with.

activities.title: Di dove sei?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You meet someone new and exchange origins and travel plans using the simple prepositions.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Hi, where are you from?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sono di Verona, ma abito in Italia da poco.
lines.en: I am from Verona, but I have been living in Italy for a short time.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: And where are you going now?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Vado a Milano con un amico.
lines.en: I am going to Milan with a friend.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Are you coming home tonight?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, torno da Milano alle nove.
lines.en: Yes, I am coming back from Milan at nine.

days.title: Le preposizioni articolate
days.objective: Fuse di, a, da, in and su with the definite articles and use the resulting forms correctly in sentences.
days.paretoFocus: From this point on every sentence with a definite article after a preposition needs a fused form, so learners who never memorise the table guess forever.
drills: Write the full grid: put the five prepositions down the side and il, lo, la, i, gli, le across the top, then fill in all thirty forms from memory.
Say where five objects in your room are, using a fused form with su or in every time.
Take five sentences you already know with a simple preposition and add a definite article, changing the preposition to its fused form.

items: Give the five forms of a + article for il, lo, la, i and gli.
Say 'the friends' house is big' in Italian and explain which two words fused.
What happens to in before the article le?

activities.title: La tabella delle preposizioni articolate
activities.intro: When di, a, da, in or su is followed by a definite article, the two words fuse into one. Learn the grid as a block: di gives del, dello, della, dei, degli, delle; a gives al, allo, alla, ai, agli, alle; da gives dal, dallo, dalla, dai, dagli, dalle; in gives nel, nello, nella, nei, negli, nelle; su gives sul, sullo, sulla, sui, sugli, sulle. Before l' the forms are dell', all', dall', nell', sull', as in all'una and nell'armadio.

items.hr: Il libro è sul tavolo.
items.en: The book is on the table.
items.note: su + il = sul

items.hr: Vado alla stazione a piedi.
items.en: I am going to the station on foot.
items.note: a + la = alla

items.hr: Esco dallo studio alle sei.
items.en: I leave the office at six.
items.note: da + lo = dallo, a + le = alle

items.hr: La casa degli amici è molto grande.
items.en: The friends' house is very big.
items.note: di + gli = degli

items.hr: I bambini giocano nei giardini.
items.en: The children play in the gardens.
items.note: in + i = nei

activities.title: Unisci preposizione e articolo
activities.intro: Practise producing the fused forms instead of keeping the two words separate.

questions.prompt: Which form joins su with the article lo?
questions.explanation: Su + lo = sullo, used before s plus consonant, z, gn, ps, x and y: sullo specchio, sullo zaino.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Metto la giacca ___ armadio.' (I put the jacket in the wardrobe.)
questions.explanation: Armadio begins with a vowel, so the article is l' and in + l' = nell'.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Il telefono è ___ borsa.' (The phone is in the bag.)
questions.explanation: In + la = nella, because borsa is feminine singular.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Parlo ___ studenti ogni giorno.' (I speak to the students every day.)
questions.explanation: A + gli = agli, and studenti takes gli because it starts with s plus consonant.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'The keys are on the table.'
questions.explanation: Su plus il fuses into sul before a masculine singular noun.

activities.title: Dove sono le mie cose?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are looking for your things and every answer needs a fused preposition.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dove sono le chiavi della macchina?
lines.en: Where are the car keys?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: They are on the kitchen table.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: And my coat?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: It is in the wardrobe, near the window.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Perfect. What time are we going out?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Alle otto, così arriviamo presto allo stadio.
lines.en: At eight, so we get to the stadium early.

days.title: C'è e ci sono
days.objective: Say what exists or is present in a place with c'è and ci sono, including the negative form.
days.paretoFocus: This is the everyday way to describe a room, a town or a fridge, and it lets you build long descriptions with almost no new grammar.
drills: Describe the room you are in with five sentences, choosing between the singular and the plural form each time.
Say three things that are not present where you are, using the negative.
Ask three questions about a friend's town starting with the existence structure.

items: When do you use the singular form and when the plural one?
Say 'there is no milk in the fridge' in Italian.
Turn 'there are two chemists here' into a question.

activities.title: C'è, ci sono, non c'è
activities.intro: To say that something exists in a place, Italian uses c'è with a singular noun and ci sono with a plural noun. The choice depends on the thing, not on the speaker. For the negative you put non in front: non c'è, non ci sono.

items.hr: In questa via c'è una farmacia.
items.en: There is a chemist in this street.
items.note: singular noun, so c'è

items.hr: Nel frigorifero ci sono due bottiglie di acqua.
items.en: There are two bottles of water in the fridge.
items.note: plural noun, so ci sono

items.hr: Oggi non c'è il sole.
items.en: There is no sun today.
items.note: negative: non before c'è

items.hr: Nella mia città non ci sono metropolitane.
items.en: There are no underground trains in my city.
items.note: plural negative

items.hr: Scusi, c'è un bagno qui vicino?
items.en: Excuse me, is there a toilet nearby?
items.note: question with rising intonation only

activities.title: Che cosa c'è?
activities.intro: Practise choosing between the singular and plural existence forms and making them negative.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'In centro ___ molti negozi.' (There are many shops in the centre.)
questions.explanation: Negozi is plural, so the plural existence form is required.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Sul tavolo ___ un bicchiere.' (There is a glass on the table.)
questions.explanation: Bicchiere is singular, and ci plus è is always written c'è with an apostrophe.

questions.prompt: Complete the negative: 'Oggi in ufficio non ___ nessuno.' (Today there is nobody in the office.)
questions.explanation: Nessuno is singular, so the singular form follows non.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Nella mia camera ___ due finestre grandi.' (My bedroom has two big windows.)
questions.explanation: Two windows is plural, so Italian uses the plural existence form.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'There is a supermarket near the station.'
questions.explanation: Singular noun takes c'è, and vicino a plus la fuses into vicino alla.

activities.title: Com'è il tuo quartiere?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Describe your neighbourhood to someone who has just moved there, using the existence structure in every answer.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Nel tuo quartiere c'è un mercato?
lines.en: Is there a market in your neighbourhood?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, c'è un mercato piccolo il sabato.
lines.en: Yes, there is a small market on Saturdays.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: And are there restaurants?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ci sono due ristoranti e tre bar.
lines.en: There are two restaurants and three cafes.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Is there a bank as well?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: No, non c'è, ma ci sono due farmacie.
lines.en: No, there is not, but there are two chemists.

days.title: La casa e i mobili
days.objective: Name the rooms of a home and the main pieces of furniture, and say what is in each room.
days.paretoFocus: Describing where you live is one of the first things anyone asks about, and the vocabulary also carries the article and preposition practice you have just learned.
drills: Walk through your home and name each room out loud with its article.
Choose three rooms and say two pieces of furniture that are in each one.
Write four sentences describing your favourite room, with size or colour adjectives that agree.

items: Name four rooms of a home with the correct article.
Say 'the mirror is in the bathroom' in Italian.
Which article does specchio take, and why?

activities.title: Le stanze e i mobili
activities.intro: A typical home has la cucina, il bagno, la camera da letto and il soggiorno. Learn each noun together with its article, because the article tells you the gender and you will need it for every fused preposition. Note lo specchio and l'armadio, which take the special article forms.

items.hr: Nel soggiorno c'è un divano grande e comodo.
items.en: In the living room there is a big comfortable sofa.
items.note: il soggiorno, il divano

items.hr: La camera da letto è piccola, ma il letto è nuovo.
items.en: The bedroom is small, but the bed is new.
items.note: adjectives agree: piccola, nuovo

items.hr: Lo specchio del bagno è vecchio.
items.en: The bathroom mirror is old.
items.note: lo before s plus consonant; di + il = del

items.hr: Metto i vestiti nell'armadio.
items.en: I put the clothes in the wardrobe.
items.note: l'armadio, so in + l' = nell'

items.hr: In cucina ci sono un tavolo, quattro sedie e il frigorifero.
items.en: In the kitchen there are a table, four chairs and the fridge.
items.note: in cucina without article is the normal fixed phrase

activities.title: In quale stanza?
activities.intro: Practise home vocabulary with the right article and the right room.

questions.prompt: Which article goes with specchio?
questions.explanation: Masculine nouns beginning with s plus consonant take lo in the singular.

questions.prompt: Where would you normally find il frigorifero?
questions.explanation: Il frigorifero is a kitchen appliance, and in cucina is the usual phrase without an article.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Dormo sempre nel mio ___ nuovo.' (I always sleep in my new bed.)
questions.explanation: Il letto is masculine singular, which is why the possessive is mio and the adjective is nuovo.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Ci sono quattro ___ intorno al tavolo.' (There are four chairs around the table.)
questions.explanation: La sedia is feminine and forms its plural in e.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'The wardrobe is in the bedroom.'
questions.explanation: Camera is feminine, so in plus la gives nella.

activities.title: La mia nuova casa
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You describe your new flat to a curious friend, naming rooms and furniture.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: What is your new home like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: It is small, but very bright.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: How many rooms are there?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ci sono la cucina, il bagno e una camera da letto.
lines.en: There are the kitchen, the bathroom and one bedroom.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: And the furniture?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ho un divano vecchio, un tavolo e un armadio grande.
lines.en: I have an old sofa, a table and a big wardrobe.

days.title: Al bar, ordinare qualcosa
days.objective: Order a drink or a snack in an Italian bar politely and ask what it costs.
days.paretoFocus: The bar is the most frequent short transaction in daily Italian life, and the same three or four formulas cover almost every visit.
drills: Order three different drinks out loud, changing the polite formula each time.
Practise saying what you want for two people in a single sentence.
Ask for the price of two items and then say you are paying.

items: Give two different ways to order a coffee politely.
Say 'a cappuccino and a croissant, please' in Italian.
How do you ask how much something costs?

activities.title: Al bar
activities.intro: In an Italian bar you order with a simple present tense verb, most often prendo, or with the phrase per me. Adding per favore or grazie is what makes it polite, so you do not need any new grammar. Note the accent in caffè, which is always written with the grave accent.

items.hr: Prendo un caffè, per favore.
items.en: I will have a coffee, please.
items.note: prendere in the present is the normal way to order

items.hr: Per me un cappuccino e un cornetto.
items.en: A cappuccino and a croissant for me.
items.note: per me plus the item, no verb needed

items.hr: Un tè freddo e un'acqua minerale, grazie.
items.en: An iced tea and a mineral water, thank you.
items.note: un'acqua with apostrophe, feminine before a vowel

items.hr: Quanto costa un panino?
items.en: How much does a sandwich cost?
items.note: costa for one thing, costano for several

items.hr: Pago alla cassa o al tavolo?
items.en: Do I pay at the till or at the table?
items.note: a + la = alla, a + il = al

activities.title: Ordinare al banco
activities.intro: Practise the fixed formulas for ordering and paying in a bar.

questions.prompt: Which sentence orders one coffee politely?
questions.explanation: Prendere in the present is the standard ordering verb; essere and avere do not work here.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Un ___ minerale, per favore.' (A mineral water, please.)
questions.explanation: Acqua is feminine and begins with a vowel, so una becomes un' with an apostrophe.

questions.prompt: Complete the polite order: 'Prendo un cappuccino, per ___.' (I will have a cappuccino, please.)
questions.explanation: Per favore is the standard polite ending of an order; per piacere is a common alternative.

questions.prompt: Complete the question: 'Quanto ___ due cornetti?' (How much are two croissants?)
questions.explanation: Two croissants is plural, so the verb takes the third person plural ending.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I will have a tea, please.'
questions.explanation: The present of prendere works as a polite order, and tè carries a grave accent.

activities.title: Un caffè al banco
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You order breakfast in a busy bar and pay for it.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Good morning, go ahead.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Buongiorno, prendo un caffè e un cornetto.
lines.en: Good morning, I will have a coffee and a croissant.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Il cornetto è alla crema o al cioccolato?
lines.en: Is the croissant with cream or with chocolate?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Alla crema, grazie. Quanto costa in tutto?
lines.en: With cream, thanks. How much is it altogether?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Three euros fifty.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ecco. Pago qui o alla cassa?
lines.en: Here you are. Do I pay here or at the till?

days.title: Al ristorante
days.objective: Book a table, understand the parts of an Italian menu and order a full meal.
days.paretoFocus: Italian menus follow a fixed order of courses, and knowing the four names lets you read any menu in the country without help.
drills: Say the four course names in order, from starter to dessert.
Invent a three course meal for yourself and order it out loud in one sentence per course.
Practise booking a table for four people for tomorrow evening at eight.

items: Name the four courses of an Italian menu in the usual order.
Say 'the bill, please' in Italian.
How do you ask for still water rather than sparkling?

activities.title: Il menù italiano
activities.intro: An Italian menu runs in a fixed order: l'antipasto, il primo, il secondo with il contorno, then il dolce. You order each course with a present tense verb, and you ask for the bill at the end with il conto. Note the accent on menù.

items.hr: Un tavolo per due, per favore.
items.en: A table for two, please.
items.note: the standard phrase on arrival

items.hr: Come antipasto prendo il prosciutto con il melone.
items.en: As a starter I will have ham with melon.
items.note: come = as, introducing the course

items.hr: Di primo vorremmo gli spaghetti al pomodoro.
items.en: For the first course we would like spaghetti with tomato.
items.note: gli before s plus consonant; a + il = al

items.hr: Di secondo prendo il pesce con un contorno di verdure.
items.en: For the main course I will have fish with a side of vegetables.
items.note: il contorno is the side dish

items.hr: Il conto, per favore. Acqua naturale o frizzante?
items.en: The bill, please. Still or sparkling water?
items.note: naturale = still, frizzante = sparkling

activities.title: Ordinare un pasto
activities.intro: Practise the course names and the standard restaurant phrases.

questions.prompt: Which course comes immediately after l'antipasto?
questions.explanation: The order is antipasto, primo, secondo with contorno, then dolce.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Vorremmo un tavolo ___ quattro persone.' (We would like a table for four people.)
questions.explanation: Per expresses the purpose or the number a thing is intended for.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Alla fine chiediamo il ___ al cameriere.' (At the end we ask the waiter for the bill.)
questions.explanation: Il conto is the bill; il menù is the list of dishes.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Per me acqua ___, senza bollicine.' (Still water for me, no bubbles.)
questions.explanation: Naturale is the standard word for still water, opposite of frizzante.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'As a main course I will have fish.'
questions.explanation: Di plus the course name is the fixed way to announce what you are ordering.

activities.title: Una cena in trattoria
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are at a small trattoria, working through the menu one course at a time.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Good evening, do you have a booking?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: No, siamo in due. C'è un tavolo libero?
lines.en: No, there are two of us. Is there a free table?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sì, accomodatevi. Che cosa prendete di primo?
lines.en: Yes, take a seat. What are you having for the first course?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Di primo prendiamo gli spaghetti al pomodoro.
lines.en: For the first course we will have spaghetti with tomato.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: And to drink?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Una bottiglia di acqua naturale, grazie.
lines.en: A bottle of still water, thank you.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Would you also like a dessert?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: No, grazie. Ci porta il conto, per favore?
lines.en: No, thank you. Could you bring us the bill, please?

days.title: Il cibo e la spesa
days.objective: Name everyday foods and ask for quantities when shopping, using un chilo di, un etto di and una bottiglia di.
days.paretoFocus: Italian shopping runs on quantity plus di plus food, so one small pattern unlocks every counter and market stall.
drills: Write a shopping list of eight foods, each with its correct article.
Ask for five of those foods with a quantity, changing the quantity word every time.
Say which two foods you buy every week and which one you never buy.

items: How do you ask for a hundred grams of cheese in Italian?
Which small word links a quantity to the food that follows?
Name three foods you would buy at a fruit and vegetable stall.

activities.title: Fare la spesa
activities.intro: To ask for an amount of food, Italian puts the quantity first, then di, then the food: un chilo di mele. Un etto is one hundred grams, and it is the normal unit at a delicatessen counter. Foods keep their article when you talk about them in general.

items.hr: Vorrei un chilo di mele, per favore.
items.en: I would like a kilo of apples, please.
items.note: quantity + di + food

items.hr: Mi dia due etti di prosciutto crudo.
items.en: Give me two hundred grams of cured ham.
items.note: un etto = 100 grams

items.hr: Compro il pane e il latte ogni mattina.
items.en: I buy bread and milk every morning.
items.note: general foods keep the definite article

items.hr: Al mercato la frutta e la verdura sono più fresche.
items.en: At the market the fruit and vegetables are fresher.
items.note: più with the grave accent

items.hr: Prendo anche una bottiglia di olio e mezzo litro di latte.
items.en: I am also taking a bottle of oil and half a litre of milk.
items.note: mezzo agrees: mezzo litro, mezza bottiglia

activities.title: Al mercato
activities.intro: Practise asking for foods with the right quantity word and linking preposition.

questions.prompt: How much is un etto?
questions.explanation: Un etto is exactly one hundred grams, the standard unit at Italian counters.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Un chilo ___ pomodori, per favore.' (A kilo of tomatoes, please.)
questions.explanation: Di always links a quantity to the thing measured.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Compro mezzo litro di ___ ogni giorno.' (I buy half a litre of milk every day.)
questions.explanation: Il latte is a masculine noun with a double t.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Al mercato la ___ costa poco in estate.' (At the market fruit is cheap in summer.)
questions.explanation: La frutta is feminine singular and takes a double t.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I would like two hundred grams of cheese.'
questions.explanation: Etto becomes etti in the plural, and di links it to the food.

activities.title: Faccio la spesa
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You buy food at a market stall, always giving a quantity.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Good morning, what can I get you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Un chilo di mele e mezzo chilo di pomodori.
lines.en: A kilo of apples and half a kilo of tomatoes.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Very good. Anything else?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, due etti di formaggio e una bottiglia di olio.
lines.en: Yes, two hundred grams of cheese and a bottle of oil.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Here you are. Is that all?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Basta così, grazie. Quanto è in tutto?
lines.en: That is all, thank you. How much is it altogether?

days.title: I soldi e i prezzi
days.objective: Ask and understand prices in euros and cents, and say how you want to pay.
days.paretoFocus: Numbers spoken fast at a till are the single hardest listening moment for beginners, and the phrases that surround them are few and fixed.
drills: Say ten prices out loud in euros and cents, including some over one hundred.
Ask the price of one item and then of several items, changing the verb form.
Practise saying you are paying by card and then in cash.

items: What changes in the verb when you ask about several items instead of one?
Say 'twelve euros forty' in Italian.
Give the Italian for paying in cash and paying by card.

activities.title: Quanto costa?
activities.intro: Prices use costare, which agrees with the thing sold: costa for one item, costano for several. Italians usually say the euros, then the cents, joined by e. To say how you pay, use pagare with in contanti or con la carta.

items.hr: Quanto costa questo libro?
items.en: How much does this book cost?
items.note: one item, so costa

items.hr: Quanto costano queste scarpe?
items.en: How much do these shoes cost?
items.note: plural item, so costano

items.hr: Costa dodici euro e quaranta centesimi.
items.en: It costs twelve euros forty cents.
items.note: euro stays the same in the plural

items.hr: Pago con la carta, non ho contanti.
items.en: I am paying by card, I have no cash.

items.hr: Questo cappotto è troppo caro, quello è più economico.
items.en: This coat is too expensive, that one is cheaper.
items.note: caro and economico agree with the noun

activities.title: Prezzi e pagamenti
activities.intro: Practise asking prices with the correct verb form and stating your method of payment.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Quanto ___ questi pantaloni?' (How much are these trousers?)
questions.explanation: Pantaloni is plural in Italian, so the verb takes the third person plural form.

questions.prompt: Which sentence means you are paying with banknotes?
questions.explanation: In contanti means in cash; con la carta means by card.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Il biglietto costa due euro e cinquanta ___.' (The ticket costs two euros fifty cents.)
questions.explanation: Centesimi is the plural word for cents; euro itself does not change in the plural.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Questa giacca è troppo ___ per me, non la compro.' (This jacket is too expensive for me.)
questions.explanation: Giacca is feminine singular, so the adjective ends in a.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'How much does this coffee cost?'
questions.explanation: One singular item takes costa, and caffè keeps its grave accent.

activities.title: Alla cassa
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You check prices in a shop and then pay at the till.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Do you need any help?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, how much does this T-shirt cost?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Quindici euro. Le magliette bianche costano dodici.
lines.en: Fifteen euros. The white T-shirts cost twelve.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Then I will take two white T-shirts.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: That is twenty four euros. How are you paying?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: I am paying by card, thank you.

days.title: In città, chiedere e dare indicazioni
days.objective: Stop someone politely to ask the way and follow simple street directions.
days.paretoFocus: Asking for directions is the classic moment when you must both produce a question and understand a stranger's fast reply, and the vocabulary is small and repetitive.
drills: Ask the way to four different places in your town, using the polite formula each time.
Give directions from your home to the nearest shop in three short sentences.
List five words that describe position, such as near, far, in front of and behind.

items: How do you politely get a stranger's attention before asking a question?
Say 'go straight on and then take the first street on the right' in Italian.
Give the Italian for opposite, near and far.

activities.title: Scusi, dov'è la stazione?
activities.intro: To ask the way, start with scusi to a stranger and then dov'è plus the place. Directions are commonly given in the polite present tense, as in prende la prima strada a destra, which you already know from the present of regular verbs. Learn the position words in pairs so you never mix them up.

items.hr: Scusi, dov'è la stazione, per favore?
items.en: Excuse me, where is the station, please?
items.note: dov'è = dove + è with apostrophe

items.hr: Va sempre dritto per cento metri.
items.en: You go straight on for a hundred metres.
items.note: sempre dritto = straight on

items.hr: Poi prende la prima strada a destra.
items.en: Then you take the first street on the right.
items.note: polite present of prendere

items.hr: La farmacia è di fronte alla banca.
items.en: The chemist is opposite the bank.
items.note: di fronte a + la = di fronte alla

items.hr: È lontano da qui? No, è molto vicino.
items.en: Is it far from here? No, it is very near.
items.note: lontano da, vicino a

activities.title: Trovare la strada
activities.intro: Practise the question forms and the direction words you will hear in the street.

questions.prompt: Which is the correct polite opening to a stranger?
questions.explanation: Scusi is the polite form, and dove plus è contracts to dov'è before the article.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Il cinema è ___ fronte alla posta.' (The cinema is opposite the post office.)
questions.explanation: The fixed phrase is di fronte a, and a plus la becomes alla.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Prenda la prima strada a ___, non a sinistra.' (Take the first street on the right.)
questions.explanation: Destra is right and sinistra is left; both are used without an article after a.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Il supermercato non è ___ da qui, sono cinque minuti a piedi.' (The supermarket is not far from here.)
questions.explanation: Lontano is followed by da, while its opposite vicino is followed by a.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'Excuse me, where is the post office?'
questions.explanation: Polite scusi opens the question and dov'è keeps its apostrophe.

activities.title: Per andare al museo?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are lost in the centre of town and ask a passer-by for the museum.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Yes, do you need something?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Excuse me, where is the town museum?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Va sempre dritto fino alla piazza.
lines.en: You go straight on as far as the square.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: E poi? Prendo a destra o a sinistra?
lines.en: And then? Do I go right or left?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Prende la seconda strada a sinistra, di fronte alla banca.
lines.en: You take the second street on the left, opposite the bank.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Is it far from here?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: No, sono dieci minuti a piedi.
lines.en: No, it is ten minutes on foot.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Perfect, thank you very much!

days.title: I mezzi di trasporto
days.objective: Say how you travel using in, a and con, and buy a ticket for a journey.
days.paretoFocus: Two tiny prepositions cover every means of transport in Italian, and getting them wrong is one of the most noticeable beginner mistakes.
drills: Say how you get to five different places, using a different means of transport each time.
Buy a return ticket out loud for a journey between two cities.
Write three sentences saying which transport you prefer and why.

items: Which preposition goes with treno, and which one goes with piedi?
Say 'a return ticket to Milan, please' in Italian.
Give the Italian for the bus, the underground and the coach.

activities.title: Come vai al lavoro?
activities.intro: With almost every vehicle Italian uses in: in treno, in autobus, in macchina, in bicicletta. The one common exception is a piedi, on foot. When you name a specific vehicle with an article you use prendere instead: prendo il treno delle otto.

items.hr: Vado al lavoro in autobus ogni mattina.
items.en: I go to work by bus every morning.
items.note: in + vehicle, no article

items.hr: Preferisco andare a piedi quando c'è il sole.
items.en: I prefer to walk when it is sunny.
items.note: a piedi is the exception

items.hr: Prendo il treno delle otto e mezza.
items.en: I take the half past eight train.
items.note: prendere + article when the vehicle is specific

items.hr: Un biglietto di andata e ritorno per Milano, per favore.
items.en: A return ticket to Milan, please.
items.note: andata e ritorno = return

items.hr: La metropolitana è più veloce della macchina.
items.en: The underground is faster than the car.
items.note: più with grave accent; di + la = della

activities.title: In treno o a piedi?
activities.intro: Practise the transport prepositions and the language of buying a ticket.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Vado in centro ___ bicicletta.' (I go to the centre by bike.)
questions.explanation: Vehicles take in without an article; only a piedi uses a.

questions.prompt: Which phrase means on foot?
questions.explanation: A piedi means walking; in piedi means standing up, which is a different idea.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Ogni giorno ___ il treno delle sette.' (Every day I take the seven o'clock train.)
questions.explanation: When the vehicle carries an article, Italian uses prendere rather than the in construction.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Un biglietto di ___ e ritorno per Firenze.' (A return ticket to Florence.)
questions.explanation: Andata is the outward leg; a one way ticket is di sola andata.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I go to work by car.'
questions.explanation: A plus il gives al, and the means of transport takes in with no article.

activities.title: Alla biglietteria
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You buy a train ticket and discuss how to reach the station.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Good morning, where are you going?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: A Bologna. Un biglietto di andata e ritorno, per favore.
lines.en: To Bologna. A return ticket, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: C'è un treno alle nove e uno alle dieci.
lines.en: There is a train at nine and one at ten.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Prendo il treno delle nove, grazie.
lines.en: I will take the nine o'clock train, thank you.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Va bene. Come arriva in stazione?
lines.en: All right. How are you getting to the station?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Vado in autobus, oppure a piedi se c'è tempo.
lines.en: I go by bus, or on foot if there is time.

days.title: Il verbo piacere
days.objective: Say what you and other people like using mi piace and mi piacciono with the full set of mi, ti, gli, le, ci and vi.
days.paretoFocus: Liking things comes up in every conversation, and piacere works backwards from English, so learning it as a pattern now prevents a mistake that otherwise lasts for years.
drills: Say five things you like, choosing the singular or plural verb correctly each time.
Say what three different people like, changing the small word in front.
Write three sentences with the negative, saying what you do not like.

items: What decides whether the verb is piace or piacciono?
Say 'we like Italian films' in Italian.
Say 'she does not like coffee' in Italian.

activities.title: Mi piace, mi piacciono
activities.intro: Piacere turns the English sentence around: the thing you like is the subject of the Italian verb, and the person who likes it comes first as an indirect object. So mi piace la pizza literally says the pizza is pleasing to me. Because the thing is the subject, the verb is piace with one thing and piacciono with several, and the person word changes: mi, ti, gli for him, le for her, ci, vi.

items.hr: Mi piace la musica italiana.
items.en: I like Italian music.
items.note: one thing, so piace

items.hr: Mi piacciono i libri di storia.
items.en: I like history books.
items.note: several things, so piacciono

items.hr: Ti piace il caffè senza zucchero?
items.en: Do you like coffee without sugar?
items.note: ti = to you

items.hr: A Marco piace il calcio, ma a Sara piacciono i film.
items.en: Marco likes football, but Sara likes films.
items.note: with a name, use a plus the name

items.hr: Non ci piace uscire tardi la sera.
items.en: We do not like going out late in the evening.
items.note: before an infinitive the verb stays singular

activities.title: Che cosa ti piace?
activities.intro: Practise choosing the singular or plural verb and the right person word.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Mi ___ gli spaghetti.' (I like spaghetti.)
questions.explanation: Gli spaghetti is plural and is the subject, so the verb takes the plural form.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly says that she likes the sea?
questions.explanation: Le means to her, and il mare is one singular subject, so the verb is piace.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Non mi ___ le città rumorose.' (I do not like noisy cities.)
questions.explanation: Le città is plural, so the verb agrees in the plural even in the negative.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Vi ___ viaggiare in treno?' (Do you all like travelling by train?)
questions.explanation: Vi means to you all, and when an infinitive follows, the verb stays in the singular.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I like Italian films a lot.'
questions.explanation: I film italiani is plural and is the subject, so piacciono agrees with it.

activities.title: Gusti e preferenze
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You talk about tastes with a friend and use piacere in every reply.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Do you like Italian cooking?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, mi piace molto, soprattutto la pizza.
lines.en: Yes, I like it a lot, especially pizza.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: And desserts? Do you like them?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Non mi piacciono i dolci troppo dolci.
lines.en: I do not like desserts that are too sweet.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A mio fratello piace il gelato al limone.
lines.en: My brother likes lemon ice cream.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Anche a noi ci piace, lo prendiamo sempre in estate.
lines.en: We like it too, we always have it in summer.

days.title: I verbi modali potere, volere, dovere
days.objective: Use the present of potere, volere and dovere with an infinitive to express ability, wish and obligation.
days.paretoFocus: Three irregular verbs plus any infinitive give you permission, plans and duties, which multiplies everything you already know by three.
drills: Write out the six present forms of each of the three modal verbs from memory.
Say three things you have to do today and three things you want to do this week.
Ask permission for three things, using the polite question form.

items: Give the io and noi forms of all three modal verbs.
What form of the second verb always follows a modal verb?
Say 'we have to leave early tomorrow' in Italian.

activities.title: Posso, voglio, devo
activities.intro: The three modal verbs are irregular but very frequent: potere (posso, puoi, può, possiamo, potete, possono), volere (voglio, vuoi, vuole, vogliamo, volete, vogliono) and dovere (devo, devi, deve, dobbiamo, dovete, devono). They are always followed by an infinitive, never by a second conjugated verb. Posso is also the normal way to ask permission.

items.hr: Posso aprire la finestra?
items.en: May I open the window?
items.note: potere for permission

items.hr: Non possiamo uscire, piove troppo.
items.en: We cannot go out, it is raining too much.

items.hr: Voglio imparare bene l'italiano.
items.en: I want to learn Italian well.
items.note: volere plus infinitive

items.hr: Devo andare dal medico alle tre.
items.en: I have to go to the doctor at three.
items.note: dovere for obligation; da + il = dal

items.hr: I bambini devono dormire presto la sera.
items.en: Children have to sleep early in the evening.
items.note: third person plural devono

activities.title: Volere, potere, dovere
activities.intro: Practise the irregular modal forms and the infinitive that follows them.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Noi ___ studiare per l'esame.' (We have to study for the exam.)
questions.explanation: The noi form of dovere is irregular and becomes dobbiamo.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is grammatically correct?
questions.explanation: A modal verb is always followed by an infinitive, not by a second conjugated verb.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Scusi, ___ pagare con la carta?' (Excuse me, can I pay by card?)
questions.explanation: The io form of potere is irregular: posso.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Loro ___ un caffè prima di partire.' (They want a coffee before leaving.)
questions.explanation: The loro form of volere is vogliono, with the gli sound spelled gli.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'We have to leave early tomorrow.'
questions.explanation: Dobbiamo is followed directly by the infinitive partire.

activities.title: Facciamo un programma
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You plan an evening with a friend, using a modal verb in every line.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Do you want to come to the cinema tonight?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Voglio venire, ma devo finire un lavoro.
lines.en: I want to come, but I have to finish a job.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: What time can you go out?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: I can go out after eight.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Allora dobbiamo prendere i biglietti adesso.
lines.en: Then we have to get the tickets now.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Va bene, però vogliamo posti vicino al centro.
lines.en: All right, but we want seats near the middle.

days.title: Il tempo e le stagioni
days.objective: Talk about the weather with fa, c'è and impersonal verbs, and name the four seasons.
days.paretoFocus: Weather is the default small talk everywhere in Italy and it uses three fixed patterns that never change.
drills: Describe today's weather in three different sentences, using a different pattern each time.
Say what the weather is usually like in each of the four seasons where you live.
Ask a friend about the weather in their city and answer for yourself.

items: Which verb introduces caldo and freddo, and which one introduces sole and vento?
Name the four seasons with their articles.
Say 'it is raining and it is cold' in Italian.

activities.title: Che tempo fa?
activities.intro: Italian weather uses three patterns. Fare goes with temperature words: fa caldo, fa freddo, fa bel tempo. C'è goes with things you can see: c'è il sole, c'è vento, c'è nebbia. Rain and snow have their own impersonal verbs: piove, nevica.

items.hr: Oggi fa molto caldo, sono trenta gradi.
items.en: Today it is very hot, it is thirty degrees.
items.note: fare with caldo and freddo

items.hr: In autunno c'è spesso vento e piove.
items.en: In autumn it is often windy and it rains.
items.note: c'è for visible weather; piove is impersonal

items.hr: In inverno nevica in montagna.
items.en: In winter it snows in the mountains.
items.note: nevicare, third person only

items.hr: In primavera fa bel tempo e le giornate sono più lunghe.
items.en: In spring the weather is nice and the days are longer.

items.hr: D'estate andiamo al mare perché fa caldo.
items.en: In summer we go to the seaside because it is hot.
items.note: d'estate = in summer; perché with acute accent

activities.title: Le quattro stagioni
activities.intro: Practise choosing the right weather pattern and naming the seasons.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Stamattina ___ freddo, mettiti la giacca.' (This morning it is cold.)
questions.explanation: Temperature words like caldo and freddo take fare, not essere.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Non esco perché ___ molto vento.' (I am not going out because it is very windy.)
questions.explanation: Visible weather such as vento, sole and nebbia takes c'è.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Prendi l'ombrello, ___ da due ore.' (Take the umbrella, it has been raining for two hours.)
questions.explanation: Rain uses the impersonal verb piovere in the third person singular, with no subject.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'La stagione dopo l'inverno è la ___.' (The season after winter is spring.)
questions.explanation: La primavera is feminine; the other seasons are l'estate, l'autunno and l'inverno.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'In summer it is hot and there is a lot of sun.'
questions.explanation: Caldo takes fa while sole takes c'è, so the two patterns appear side by side.

activities.title: Che tempo fa da te?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You compare the weather in two cities with a friend who lives elsewhere.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Che tempo fa oggi a casa tua?
lines.en: What is the weather like at your place today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Fa freddo e c'è un po' di nebbia.
lines.en: It is cold and there is a bit of fog.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Qui invece c'è il sole, ma tira vento.
lines.en: Here on the other hand it is sunny, but it is windy.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Beato te. In inverno da noi piove sempre.
lines.en: Lucky you. In winter it always rains where we are.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E in estate? Fa molto caldo?
lines.en: And in summer? Is it very hot?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, in estate fa caldo e le giornate sono lunghe.
lines.en: Yes, in summer it is hot and the days are long.

days.title: I vestiti e i colori
days.objective: Name clothes and colours and make the colour adjective agree with the item of clothing.
days.paretoFocus: Clothes plus colours is the clearest everyday place to practise adjective agreement, and it is also exactly what shop assistants ask you about.
drills: Describe what you are wearing right now, giving the colour of each item.
List five colours that change their ending and two that never change.
Describe what two people near you are wearing, in complete sentences.

items: Which two common colours never change their ending?
Say 'I am wearing a white shirt and black shoes' in Italian.
Give the plural of la scarpa nera and il pantalone verde.

activities.title: Vestiti e colori
activities.intro: Colour adjectives follow the noun and agree with it: una gonna rossa, dei pantaloni rossi. Colours ending in o have four forms, colours ending in e have two, and blu, rosa and viola never change at all. Remember that pantaloni is plural in Italian even when English uses a singular idea.

items.hr: Porto una camicia bianca e una gonna nera.
items.en: I am wearing a white shirt and a black skirt.
items.note: adjectives agree: bianca, nera

items.hr: I miei pantaloni sono grigi e le scarpe sono marroni.
items.en: My trousers are grey and my shoes are brown.
items.note: pantaloni is always plural

items.hr: Mi metto il cappotto verde perché fa freddo.
items.en: I am putting on the green coat because it is cold.
items.note: verde has only two forms, verde and verdi

items.hr: Questa maglietta blu è troppo grande.
items.en: This blue T-shirt is too big.
items.note: blu never changes

items.hr: Posso provare le scarpe rosse, per favore?
items.en: May I try on the red shoes, please?
items.note: plural feminine, so rosse

activities.title: Di che colore è?
activities.intro: Practise agreeing colours with clothes and using the shop phrases that go with them.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Compro due magliette ___.' (I am buying two white T-shirts.)
questions.explanation: Magliette is feminine plural, so the adjective takes the feminine plural ending.

questions.prompt: Which colour never changes its ending?
questions.explanation: Blu, rosa and viola are invariable and stay the same in all four forms.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Il mio cappotto è ___, come la notte.' (My coat is black, like the night.)
questions.explanation: Cappotto is masculine singular, so the adjective ends in o.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Vorrei provare questi ___ grigi, taglia quarantadue.' (I would like to try on these grey trousers.)
questions.explanation: Italian treats trousers as a plural noun, which is why questi and grigi are plural too.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I am wearing a red skirt.'
questions.explanation: Gonna is feminine singular, so rosso becomes rossa after the noun.

activities.title: In un negozio di abbigliamento
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You try on clothes and discuss sizes and colours with the assistant.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Good morning, are you looking for anything in particular?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, I am looking for a white shirt.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: What size do you take?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: La media. Posso provare anche quella azzurra?
lines.en: Medium. May I also try the light blue one?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Certo, il camerino è in fondo a destra.
lines.en: Of course, the fitting room is at the back on the right.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Grazie. Avete anche i pantaloni neri?
lines.en: Thank you. Do you also have the black trousers?

days.title: Il corpo e la salute
days.objective: Name the main parts of the body and say what hurts using mi fa male and mi fanno male.
days.paretoFocus: Explaining a symptom is a situation you cannot avoid or paraphrase, and the structure reuses the piacere pattern you have just learned.
drills: Name eight parts of the body out loud with their articles.
Say three complaints, using the singular form twice and the plural form once.
Practise a short visit to the doctor: greet, say what hurts, say since when.

items: What decides whether you say fa male or fanno male?
Say 'my legs hurt' in Italian.
Give the Italian for a temperature, a cough and a sore throat.

activities.title: Mi fa male la testa
activities.intro: To say that something hurts, Italian uses fare male with the same person words as piacere: mi, ti, gli, le, ci, vi. The body part is the subject, so it is fa male with one part and fanno male with several. Note that Italian says la testa, not my head, because the person word already shows whose it is.

items.hr: Mi fa male la testa da stamattina.
items.en: My head has been hurting since this morning.
items.note: one body part, so fa male

items.hr: Mi fanno male le gambe dopo la corsa.
items.en: My legs hurt after the run.
items.note: plural body part, so fanno male

items.hr: Ho mal di gola e un po' di febbre.
items.en: I have a sore throat and a slight temperature.
items.note: mal di plus body part is a fixed phrase

items.hr: Ti fa male lo stomaco?
items.en: Does your stomach hurt?
items.note: lo stomaco takes lo because of s plus consonant

items.hr: Devo andare dal medico, non sto bene.
items.en: I have to go to the doctor, I do not feel well.
items.note: da + il = dal; stare bene = to feel well

activities.title: Dal medico
activities.intro: Practise describing symptoms with the correct verb number and body vocabulary.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Mi ___ male i denti.' (My teeth hurt.)
questions.explanation: I denti is plural and is the subject, so the verb takes the plural form.

questions.prompt: Which article goes with stomaco?
questions.explanation: Stomaco begins with s plus consonant, so the masculine article is lo.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Ho ___ di gola e non riesco a parlare bene.' (I have a sore throat.)
questions.explanation: The fixed phrase is avere mal di plus the body part, with male shortened to mal.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Non sto bene, ho trentotto di ___.' (I am not well, I have a temperature of thirty eight.)
questions.explanation: La febbre is feminine and is spelled with a double b.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'My back hurts today.'
questions.explanation: La schiena is one singular subject, so the verb stays fa male.

activities.title: Non mi sento bene
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You describe your symptoms to a doctor using the fare male pattern.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Good morning, what is the problem?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Mi fa male la gola da tre giorni.
lines.en: My throat has been hurting for three days.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Do you also have a temperature?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, e mi fanno male anche le orecchie.
lines.en: Yes, and my ears hurt as well.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Deve riposare e bere molta acqua.
lines.en: You have to rest and drink plenty of water.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Va bene. Posso andare al lavoro domani?
lines.en: All right. Can I go to work tomorrow?

days.title: La routine quotidiana e i verbi riflessivi
days.objective: Describe your daily routine using reflexive verbs with mi, ti, si, ci, vi and si.
days.paretoFocus: Almost every verb about getting up, washing and going to bed is reflexive in Italian, so a whole day of talking depends on this one pattern.
drills: Describe your morning from waking up to leaving the house, in five sentences.
Say the same routine for a friend, changing the reflexive word to the third person.
Write three sentences about your evening, adding a time expression to each.

items: Where does the reflexive word go in a normal statement?
Say 'I get up at seven and have a shower' in Italian.
Give the six reflexive words in order.

activities.title: La mia giornata
activities.intro: A reflexive verb sends the action back to the person doing it, and Italian marks this with a small pronoun placed before the verb: mi and ti in the singular, ci and vi in the plural, si for both third persons. The infinitive ends in si, as in svegliarsi, and the verb itself keeps its normal endings. When a modal verb comes first, the reflexive word usually stays in front: mi devo alzare.

items.hr: Mi sveglio alle sette e mi alzo subito.
items.en: I wake up at seven and get up straight away.
items.note: svegliarsi, alzarsi

items.hr: Poi mi lavo, mi vesto e faccio colazione.
items.en: Then I wash, get dressed and have breakfast.
items.note: lavarsi, vestirsi

items.hr: Mio fratello si sveglia sempre tardi.
items.en: My brother always wakes up late.
items.note: third person singular si

items.hr: Ci riposiamo un'ora dopo pranzo.
items.en: We rest for an hour after lunch.
items.note: riposarsi, noi form ci

items.hr: La sera mi addormento davanti alla televisione.
items.en: In the evening I fall asleep in front of the television.
items.note: addormentarsi, with double d spelled carefully

activities.title: Verbi riflessivi
activities.intro: Practise placing the reflexive word correctly and matching it to the subject.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Anna ___ veste in fretta la mattina.' (Anna gets dressed quickly in the morning.)
questions.explanation: The third person singular reflexive word is si, for both lui and lei.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is correct?
questions.explanation: The reflexive word comes before the conjugated verb, and the verb keeps its normal ending.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Noi ___ svegliamo presto ogni giorno.' (We wake up early every day.)
questions.explanation: The reflexive word for noi is ci.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'La sera mi ___ i denti prima di dormire.' (In the evening I brush my teeth before sleeping.)
questions.explanation: Lavarsi i denti is the normal phrase, and the verb keeps the regular io ending.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I get up at seven every morning.'
questions.explanation: Mi precedes the verb, and a plus le gives alle before a plural hour.

activities.title: A che ora ti alzi?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You compare daily routines with a colleague, using reflexive verbs throughout.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A che ora ti svegli la mattina?
lines.en: What time do you wake up in the morning?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Mi sveglio alle sei e mezza, ma mi alzo alle sette.
lines.en: I wake up at half past six, but I get up at seven.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Io invece mi alzo subito e mi faccio la doccia.
lines.en: I on the other hand get up straight away and have a shower.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Anche noi ci laviamo prima di colazione.
lines.en: We wash before breakfast too.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E la sera a che ora vi addormentate?
lines.en: And in the evening what time do you fall asleep?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Di solito mi addormento verso le undici.
lines.en: I usually fall asleep around eleven.

days.title: Il passato prossimo con avere
days.objective: Form the past tense of regular verbs with avere plus the past participle and use it to say what you did.
days.paretoFocus: The passato prossimo is the everyday past tense in spoken Italian, and most verbs build it with avere, so this one pattern opens up talking about anything that already happened.
drills: Write the participle of ten regular verbs, three of each conjugation plus one extra.
Say five things you did yesterday, all with verbs that take avere.
Turn three of those sentences into the negative and three into questions.

items: Give the three regular participle endings for are, ere and ire verbs.
Say 'yesterday I ate at a restaurant' in Italian.
Where does non go in a past tense sentence?

activities.title: Ho parlato, ho finito
activities.intro: The passato prossimo has two parts: the present of the auxiliary and the past participle. Most verbs take avere. Regular participles are easy: parlare gives parlato, credere gives creduto, finire gives finito. With avere the participle does not change its ending, and non goes in front of the auxiliary.

items.hr: Ieri ho parlato con il direttore.
items.en: Yesterday I spoke to the manager.
items.note: parlare, so parlato

items.hr: Abbiamo mangiato in un ristorante vicino al mare.
items.en: We ate at a restaurant near the sea.
items.note: mangiare, so mangiato

items.hr: Hai finito i compiti?
items.en: Have you finished your homework?
items.note: finire, so finito

items.hr: Non ho dormito bene stanotte.
items.en: I did not sleep well last night.
items.note: non goes before the auxiliary

items.hr: I ragazzi hanno giocato tutto il pomeriggio.
items.en: The boys played all afternoon.
items.note: with avere the participle never changes

activities.title: Che cosa hai fatto ieri?
activities.intro: Practise building the past tense with avere and regular participles.

questions.prompt: What is the past participle of credere?
questions.explanation: Regular ere verbs form their participle in uto.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Loro ___ studiato tutta la sera.' (They studied all evening.)
questions.explanation: The auxiliary agrees with the subject, and the loro form of avere is hanno.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Ieri noi ___ mangiato una pizza enorme.' (Yesterday we ate a huge pizza.)
questions.explanation: The noi form of the auxiliary is abbiamo, and the participle stays unchanged.

questions.prompt: Complete with the participle: 'Ho ___ tre ore al telefono.' (I spent three hours on the phone, from passare.)
questions.explanation: Passare is a regular are verb, so its participle ends in ato.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I did not sleep well last night.'
questions.explanation: Non stands in front of the auxiliary, never between it and the participle.

activities.title: Una giornata piena
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You tell a friend what you did yesterday, using only verbs that take avere.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: What did you do yesterday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ho lavorato fino alle sei e poi ho studiato.
lines.en: I worked until six and then I studied.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Did you eat at home?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: No, abbiamo mangiato una pizza con Luca.
lines.en: No, we ate a pizza with Luca.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E dopo avete guardato la partita?
lines.en: And afterwards did you watch the match?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, ma non ho dormito abbastanza.
lines.en: Yes, but I did not sleep enough.

days.title: Il participio passato irregolare
days.objective: Recognise and produce the most common irregular past participles such as fatto, detto, visto, preso and scritto.
days.paretoFocus: The verbs you use most often are exactly the ones with irregular participles, so a short list of about a dozen forms covers most of what you will ever say about the past.
drills: Write the participle of fare, dire, vedere, prendere, scrivere, leggere, bere, aprire, chiudere and mettere.
Cover the list and say each participle from its infinitive, then the other way round.
Use five of the irregular participles in sentences about your own week.

items: Give the participles of fare, dire and vedere.
Say 'I read an interesting book' in Italian.
Which verb gives the participle preso?

activities.title: Participi irregolari
activities.intro: Many high frequency verbs form their participle irregularly and must be learned one by one: fare gives fatto, dire gives detto, vedere gives visto, prendere gives preso, scrivere gives scritto, leggere gives letto, bere gives bevuto, aprire gives aperto, chiudere gives chiuso, mettere gives messo. They still take avere in the same way as regular verbs. Watch the double consonants, which are part of the spelling.

items.hr: Ho fatto la spesa e ho letto il giornale.
items.en: I did the shopping and read the newspaper.
items.note: fare gives fatto; leggere gives letto

items.hr: Che cosa hai detto al telefono?
items.en: What did you say on the phone?
items.note: dire gives detto

items.hr: Ieri sera abbiamo visto un bel film.
items.en: Yesterday evening we saw a good film.
items.note: vedere gives visto

items.hr: Ho preso il treno delle sette e ho bevuto un caffè in stazione.
items.en: I took the seven o'clock train and drank a coffee at the station.
items.note: prendere gives preso; bere gives bevuto

items.hr: Marta ha scritto tre messaggi e ha chiuso il computer.
items.en: Marta wrote three messages and closed the computer.
items.note: scrivere gives scritto; chiudere gives chiuso

activities.title: Dall'infinito al participio
activities.intro: Practise producing the irregular participles of the verbs you use most.

questions.prompt: What is the past participle of prendere?
questions.explanation: Prendere is irregular and gives preso, with a single s.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Ieri ho ___ una lettera a mia madre.' (Yesterday I wrote a letter to my mother.)
questions.explanation: Scrivere gives the irregular participle scritto with a double t.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Stamattina ho ___ due caffè.' (This morning I drank two coffees, from bere.)
questions.explanation: Bere comes from an older form bevere, which is why the participle is bevuto.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Abbiamo ___ la finestra perché faceva caldo.' (We opened the window, from aprire.)
questions.explanation: Aprire is irregular and gives aperto, not aprito.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'Yesterday evening we saw a good film.'
questions.explanation: Vedere gives visto, and bello shortens to bel before a masculine noun.

activities.title: Ma che cosa hai combinato?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend asks about your day and every answer needs an irregular participle.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hai visto il messaggio di Giulia?
lines.en: Did you see Giulia's message?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, I read it this morning.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: What did you say to her?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ho scritto che domani non posso uscire.
lines.en: I wrote that tomorrow I cannot go out.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Perché? Che cosa hai fatto oggi?
lines.en: Why? What did you do today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ho preso il treno alle sei e ho lavorato tutto il giorno.
lines.en: I took the six o'clock train and worked all day.

days.title: Il passato prossimo con essere
days.objective: Choose essere as the auxiliary for verbs of movement, change of state and all reflexives, and make the participle agree with the subject.
days.paretoFocus: The agreement of the participle after essere is the first thing an examiner checks, and it is decided by one clear rule rather than by feel.
drills: List eight verbs that take essere and say why each one belongs to the group.
Say four sentences about your own movements yesterday, making the participle agree with yourself.
Repeat the same four sentences for a group of people, changing the ending to i or e.

items: Which three families of verbs take essere?
Say 'we left at eight' about a group of women, with correct agreement.
What ending does the participle take when the subject is a mixed group?

activities.title: Sono andato, sono andata
activities.intro: Verbs of movement such as andare, venire, partire, arrivare, entrare, uscire and tornare take essere, and so do verbs of change of state such as nascere, morire, diventare and restare, plus every reflexive verb. With essere the participle agrees with the subject: o for a man, a for a woman, i for a masculine or mixed group, e for an all female group. All other verbs keep avere.

items.hr: Ieri sono andato al mercato.
items.en: Yesterday I went to the market.
items.note: a man speaking, so andato

items.hr: Maria è tornata a casa tardi.
items.en: Maria came home late.
items.note: feminine subject, so tornata

items.hr: Siamo partiti alle otto di mattina.
items.en: We left at eight in the morning.
items.note: mixed or masculine group, so partiti

items.hr: Le mie amiche sono uscite insieme.
items.en: My friends went out together.
items.note: all female group, so uscite

items.hr: Mi sono alzata presto e mi sono lavata.
items.en: I got up early and washed.
items.note: reflexives always take essere and agree

activities.title: Essere o avere?
activities.intro: Pick the right auxiliary, then fix the ending of the participle to match who is speaking.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Anna ___ arrivata alle nove.' (Anna arrived at nine.)
questions.explanation: Arrivare is a verb of movement, so it takes essere, and the participle agrees with Anna.

questions.prompt: Which sentence has the correct agreement for a group of women?
options: Le ragazze sono andati al cinema.
Le ragazze sono andate al cinema.
Le ragazze hanno andate al cinema.
Le ragazze è andata al cinema.
questions.answer: Le ragazze sono andate al cinema.
questions.explanation: Andare takes essere, and an all female plural subject requires the participle to end in e.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Noi ___ usciti presto ieri sera.' (We went out early yesterday evening.)
questions.explanation: Uscire is a movement verb, so the auxiliary is essere in the noi form.

questions.prompt: Complete the participle: 'Sara si è ___ alle sei e mezza.' (Sara got up at half past six, from alzarsi.)
questions.explanation: Reflexive verbs take essere and the participle agrees with the feminine singular subject.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'We left at eight in the morning.'
questions.explanation: Partire takes essere and the participle ends in i for a masculine or mixed group.

activities.title: Dove sei andato ieri?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You explain your movements yesterday and every verb chooses its auxiliary correctly.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Where did you go yesterday afternoon?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sono andato in centro con mia sorella.
lines.en: I went to the centre with my sister.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: What time did you get back?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: We got back around seven.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E stamattina ti sei alzato presto?
lines.en: And this morning did you get up early?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: No, mi sono svegliato tardi e sono uscito di corsa.
lines.en: No, I woke up late and rushed out.

days.title: Raccontare il fine settimana
days.objective: Tell a short connected story about your weekend, mixing both auxiliaries and ordering events with time expressions.
days.paretoFocus: Every Monday conversation and every speaking test starts with what you did at the weekend, so a rehearsed three sentence answer is worth more than any list of rules.
drills: Tell the story of your last weekend in six sentences, using at least two verbs with essere.
Add a time word to each sentence: prima, poi, dopo, alla fine.
Retell the same weekend about you and a friend, changing every verb to the noi form.

items: Give four words that put past events in order.
Say 'first I got up, then I went out' in Italian with correct agreement.
Which auxiliary do restare and diventare take?

activities.title: Il mio fine settimana
activities.intro: To narrate you need the past tense plus a few ordering words: prima, poi, dopo, alla fine. Keep checking the auxiliary as you go, because a story normally mixes both. Time expressions such as sabato mattina and domenica pomeriggio go at the start of the sentence.

items.hr: Sabato mattina mi sono alzata tardi.
items.en: On Saturday morning I got up late.
items.note: a woman speaking, so alzata

items.hr: Prima ho fatto colazione, poi sono uscita.
items.en: First I had breakfast, then I went out.
items.note: avere with fare, essere with uscire

items.hr: Nel pomeriggio abbiamo visitato un museo.
items.en: In the afternoon we visited a museum.
items.note: visitare takes avere

items.hr: Domenica siamo rimasti a casa perché ha piovuto.
items.en: On Sunday we stayed at home because it rained.
items.note: rimanere gives rimasto and takes essere

items.hr: Alla fine è stato un fine settimana tranquillo.
items.en: In the end it was a quiet weekend.
items.note: essere gives the participle stato

activities.title: Metti in ordine il racconto
activities.intro: Practise narrating past events in order with the correct auxiliary each time.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Domenica ___ rimasti in casa tutto il giorno.' (On Sunday we stayed at home all day.)
questions.explanation: Rimanere expresses staying in a state, so it takes essere and the participle agrees.

questions.prompt: Which word introduces the last event of a story?
questions.explanation: Alla fine marks the closing event; prima and poi order the earlier ones.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Sabato sera ___ andati a ballare con gli amici.' (On Saturday evening we went dancing with friends.)
questions.explanation: Andare is a movement verb, so it takes essere and the participle ends in i.

questions.prompt: Complete the participle: 'La domenica è ___ molto tranquilla.' (Sunday was very quiet, from essere.)
questions.explanation: Essere has the participle stato, which agrees with the feminine singular subject.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'In the afternoon we visited a museum.'
questions.explanation: Visitare takes avere, so the participle stays unchanged, and in plus il gives nel.

activities.title: Com'è andato il fine settimana?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You tell a colleague about your weekend as a connected story.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Allora, com'è andato il fine settimana?
lines.en: So, how did your weekend go?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Bene. Sabato mi sono alzato tardi e poi sono uscito.
lines.en: Good. On Saturday I got up late and then I went out.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Where did you go?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Prima abbiamo pranzato in centro, dopo abbiamo visitato una mostra.
lines.en: First we had lunch in the centre, afterwards we visited an exhibition.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: And on Sunday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Domenica siamo rimasti a casa perché ha piovuto tutto il giorno.
lines.en: On Sunday we stayed at home because it rained all day.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: So a quiet weekend.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, alla fine è stato proprio riposante.
